apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is limited, with 20 restaurants, 5 bars and cafés, and 2 discos in the village. The Jimmy Beach Bar at mid-mountain offers a convenient stop for drinks before the final descent. The scene is quiet rather than lively, with no cinema, theatre, casino, or concert hall.
resortCharacterDescription: Savognin is a traditional alpine village with the Julia River flowing through it under ancient stone bridges. The village has three churches, a museum, and a few shops and hotels. The centre is attractive but is cut through by the main road to St Moritz. The resort is small and compact, with most amenities within walking distance. The village is charming and authentic, not purpose-built.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts: 2 gondolas, 1 high-speed six-pack chairlift, and 9 surface lifts. Recent investment has improved access from the valley. The Piz Cartas area is served only by draglifts, which may be a consideration for snowboarders. The base and mid-mountain are served by modern gondolas. Total lift capacity is 13,900 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Savognin is a standalone ski area not directly linked by lifts to other resorts. Nearby ski areas include Radons (5.6 km), Colms da Parsonz (5.3 km), Tiefencastel (7.3 km), Bad Alvaneu (8.4 km), Filisur (9.8 km), Tschitta (8.8 km), Lenzerheide (13.9 km), Plang Bi (14.8 km), and Obermutten (12 km). It is part of the Surselva ski region, which also includes Laax, Brigels, and Obersaxen, but these are separate resorts with their own lift passes.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits in a north-facing natural bowl below the Kalkkögel peaks, with most terrain above 1,600 metres. The main jumping-off point is at Kreuzjoch (2,136 m), from which a network of wide red runs snakes down the mountain, interspersed with a long blue run that descends to the valley. Blue pistes span the full vertical from the Sennjoch summit (2,240 m) to the base, while a short red and black option near the Panoramabahn provides variety. The lower section offers tree-lined runs and a fast blue track back to the gondola station. The Galtbergbahn serves a separate, plush sector with its own trails.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes deep powder zones between small trees on the main slope, with options off skiers' left from the Sennjoch gondola leading into open fields and forested sections. The Free Nature Area is a designated freeride zone with spectacular slopes. Guided trips and avalanche safety courses are available for exploring the backcountry. Tree runs from the middle station down to the Froneben area provide additional off-piste opportunities, and the resort is known for varied terrain including steep slopes and open fields.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs €51–€58.50 (depending on season), with children's tickets at €25.50–€29.80 and youth tickets around €40.80. The Ski+City Pass Innsbruck Stubai, starting at €147 for two days, covers Schlick 2000 plus 12 other ski areas and 22 attractions, including public transport. A four-day Stubai Glacier pass also includes Schlick 2000. Accommodation in Fulpmes is generally cheaper than at the glacier resorts, and the free ski bus from the village reduces transport costs.
familyDescription: The BIG Family Kinderland at the Froneben mid-station (1,350 m) is a flat, separate area with snow carousels, conveyor belts, and a play and warming room supervised by mascot Big Ron. Children under eight ski for free when accompanied by an adult, and the resort caters for infants from three months old in the play rooms. The nursery slopes are wide and gentle, and the free ski bus connects the village to the gondola station. A toboggan run and snow park add extra family activities.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and centred on traditional Tyrolean huts and a handful of bars. Leo's Stadl offers live music and hearty Austrian fare, while Das Corso is a friendly bar in the village centre. Other options include Cafe Tatort and Caramba Bar. Nine traditional lodges and huts on the mountain serve food and drink, with a cosy, family-friendly atmosphere rather than a party scene. The village of Fulpmes is quiet in the evenings, with a few restaurants and a kebab shop.
resortCharacterDescription: Fulpmes is a traditional Tyrolean village with a history of iron and silver mining, reflected in the local Stubai tool brand. The village is compact and walkable, with a baroque church and a blacksmith museum. The setting is a natural high valley at the foot of the Kalkkögel, often called the 'Tyrolean Dolomites' for their dramatic peaks. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains a working Tyrolean character with wooden chalets and a relaxed pace.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 11 modern lifts with an uphill capacity of 15,000 people per hour. The Galtbergbahn is a 10-person gondola opened in 2023, the first of its kind in the Stubai Valley. The Kreuzjochbahn is a two-section 6-person gondola, and the Zirmachbahn is a 6-person chairlift with a protective dome and heated seats. Other lifts include the Sennjochbahn (4-person chairlift), Panoramabahn (4-person chairlift), and several T-bars and magic carpets. All lifts run from 8:30 to 16:30 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Schlick 2000 is one of four ski areas in the Stubai Valley, alongside the Stubai Glacier, Elferbahnen Neustift, and Serlesbahnen Mieders. A single Stubai Valley ski pass covers all four areas. It is also part of the Ski+City Pass Innsbruck Stubai, which grants access to 13 ski resorts around Innsbruck, 22 city attractions, and local public transport. The Stubai Glacier is a short bus ride up the valley and offers 65 km of glacier runs from mid-October to June.

terrainDescription: The terrain on the Schmittenhöhe is characterized by open snowfields at the summit with panoramic views over the Zeller See and the Hohe Tauern, transitioning to tree-lined trails on the lower slopes. The piste network consists predominantly of intermediate-friendly blue and red runs, with a handful of steeper black runs including the Trass, one of the longest black runs in the Salzburg region at 4 km. Dedicated beginner learning zones are located at mid-mountain (Sonnkogel) and the Areitbahn sector, away from faster traffic. The longest run is 6 km, and the total vertical drop is 1,240 m.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste on the Schmittenhöhe is limited but accessible, primarily on north-facing slopes below the summit where snow can hold well after storms. Between marked pistes, skiers and snowboarders can find natural gullies, open faces, and short powder sections, particularly on north-facing aspects. The lower tree-lined areas offer good options in poor visibility. For more extensive off-piste and freeride terrain, the Kitzsteinhorn glacier at Kaprun (linked via the Zell am See-Kaprun ski area) provides wider glacier bowls, steeper faces, and five marked freeride routes.
familyDescription: The resort offers dedicated beginner areas at Sonnkogel and Areitbahn, away from faster traffic, with gentle gradients and carpet lifts. The ski school includes Schmidolin's Kids Club for children from age four, with English-speaking instructors available. Off-slope activities include a toboggan run from Sonnkogel back to the valley, an ice skating rink on the frozen lake, and the Zell am See leisure centre with pools and waterslides. The pedestrianised town centre is safe for children, and several family-focused hotels offer kids' clubs and flexible dining. The Zell am See-Kaprun ski area also includes the Maiskogel family mountain with a dedicated beginner park.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Zell am See centres on the pedestrianised old town and lakeside promenade, with cosy bars and restaurants offering traditional Austrian cuisine and views over the frozen lake. Mountain huts on the Schmittenhöhe have sunny terraces. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy, with options ranging from traditional wine bars to more lively venues with live music. The town also offers winter hiking, spa facilities, and occasional events on the lake.
resortCharacterDescription: Zell am See is a historic town with stately traditional architecture, set on a peninsula jutting into the Zeller See lake. The lakeside promenade and pedestrianised old town give it a relaxed, sophisticated atmosphere. It is not a purpose-built ski station but an established year-round town, with a charming, picture-postcard character that combines alpine tradition with modern amenities.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system on the Schmittenhöhe consists of 28 lifts, including the world's only designer gondola from Porsche Design, high-speed six-person chairlifts, gondolas, and surface lifts. The original summit cable car from 1927 has been renovated multiple times. The network is modern and efficient, with snowmaking covering 77 km (100% of pistes). The direct Zell-Kaprun gondola connection provides seamless access to the Maiskogel and Kitzsteinhorn glacier areas.
linkedResortsDescription: The Schmittenhöhe is part of the Zell am See-Kaprun ski area, which also includes the Maiskogel family mountain and the Kitzsteinhorn glacier. A single Ski ALPIN CARD further connects to the Skicircus Saalbach Hinterglemm Leogang Fieberbrunn, creating a combined 408 km of pistes served by 121 lifts. The direct Zell-Kaprun gondola link and the zellamseeXpress to the Glemmtal provide seamless access between these areas.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Alpe di Mera consists of wide, gentle slopes on soft ridges, with runs descending from Cima Camparient at 1,800m to the valley at 600m. The slopes are predominantly south-facing and sunny. The beginner area 'Campo' covers about 400m and is served by a ski lift and treadmills. There are red runs for more advanced skiers, such as the Camparient slope. The resort also has a snowpark and a baby snow park. The slopes are groomed and have artificial snowmaking.
familyDescription: Alpe di Mera is a car-free pedestrian resort accessed by cable car, making it safe for families. There is a dedicated beginner area called 'Campo' with a ski lift and treadmills, and a baby snow park for young children. A toboggan area and playgrounds provide off-slope activities. Three ski schools offer lessons for children and adults. The slopes are wide and gentle, suitable for beginners and families.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has several mountain huts and bars, including Rifugio Camparient which serves local dishes like polenta concia and risotto ai funghi, and Bar Restaurant La Capanna. There are also bars like Campo Bar and Al Boschetto Bar. The resort hosts events that create a lively atmosphere, but the overall après-ski scene is low-key and focused on dining and relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Alpe di Mera is a car-free pedestrian resort located on an alpine pasture at 1,500m. Access is by a two-person chairlift from Scopello, eliminating car traffic. The village consists of mountain huts, lodgings, and restaurants scattered among meadows and forests. It offers a quiet, traditional alpine atmosphere with views of the Monte Rosa massif. The resort is part of the Alta Valsesia Natural Park.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes four chairlifts, one ski lift, and a treadmill. The main access from Scopello is a two-person chairlift that takes about 20 minutes. The resort has invested in artificial snowmaking, with recent extensions on the M1a-Camparient Alto piste and snowmaking on Pianaccia and Mera-Scopello slopes. The lifts are well-maintained and serve the slopes.
linkedResortsDescription: Alpe di Mera is a satellite ski area within the Monterosa Ski group, which includes other resorts in the Valsesia and Monte Rosa region such as Alagna, Gressoney, and Champoluc. However, the slopes are not directly connected by lifts; it is a separate area with its own lift system. The Monterosa Ski pass provides access to the wider area.

terrainDescription: The terrain is a high-altitude plateau (1,700–2,350 m) with wide, open slopes that are sunny and sheltered. The runs are gentle, with red runs feeling like blues, and steep pitches are virtually nonexistent. The plateau has 60 km of pistes (100% snowmaking coverage) that wind between mountain huts and lift stations, offering long flowing runs with views of the Sassolungo Group and Sciliar massif. The slopes are broad and forgiving, ideal for relaxed cruising.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited on the plateau itself, but the surrounding terrain offers excellent ski touring opportunities. Popular routes lead towards Sasso Piatto and Denti di Terrarossa, with manageable gradients suitable for newcomers to ski touring. The open plateau provides a safe environment for gentle backcountry exploration, though there are no steep couloirs or extreme off-piste descents.
valueDescription: A weekday adult lift ticket costs €80, with a season pass at €820 (adult) and €270 (child) according to Ikon Pass pricing. The resort is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, allowing access to 1,200 km of slopes with a single pass. Accommodation on the plateau tends to be upscale, but nearby villages like Siusi and Ortisei offer more budget-friendly options. The South Tyrol Guest Pass provides free public transport.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly with two ski schools offering expert instruction for children. The slopes are broad, sunny, and gentle, with a dedicated kids' fun park and the Snowpark Seiser Alm for freestyle. The fairy tale-themed Seiser Alm Ronda features interactive elements like the Witches' Flight escape game. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation allows easy access to slopes. Beyond skiing, there are cross-country trails, snowshoeing, tobogganing, and horse-drawn sleigh rides. The plateau is car-free during the day, ensuring safety.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is understated, with evenings centered on dining at mountain huts serving South Tyrolean cuisine such as speck dumplings and apple strudel. Notable huts include Zallinger Hütte and Rauchhütte. Some huts host evening dinners accessible by snowcat or sleigh. The nearby villages of Kastelruth, Seis, and Völs offer boutique shops and wellness hotels with spas. There are no lively bars or nightclubs; the atmosphere is relaxed and focused on good food and scenery.
resortCharacterDescription: Alpe di Siusi is not a traditional village but a high-altitude alpine meadow, the largest in Europe, with scattered hotels and mountain huts. The plateau is car-free during the day, preserving a peaceful, natural atmosphere. The base villages (Siusi, Ortisei) have traditional South Tyrolean architecture with cobbled streets and historic buildings. The resort is purpose-built for skiing but blends into the landscape, with modern ski-in/ski-out hotels offering panoramic views.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 28 lifts (including gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts) that are modern and well-maintained. Many lifts have child safety bars and easy-access loading. The Consorzio Impianti a Fune manages 41 companies and has invested in solar energy and photovoltaic panels to power lifts. The lifts connect the plateau to Val Gardena, and the area has 100% snowmaking coverage. Queues can be long during peak holidays.
linkedResortsDescription: Alpe di Siusi is directly linked to the Val Gardena ski area (Ortisei, Santa Cristina, Selva), forming a combined 181 km of pistes with 79 lifts. It is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which includes 12 ski areas and 1,200 km of slopes. The Sellaronda circuit, a 40 km loop around the Sella massif, is accessible via Val Gardena. Other nearby areas like Alta Badia, Arabba, and Cortina are reachable by road or via the Dolomiti Superski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 30 km of slopes, with 57% advanced and 19% expert runs. The main runs from the gondola back to the base are steep but well groomed. The longest run is 3.3 km, and there is a 5 km descent from Sedel na Kanin to Sella Nevea with a 1,200 m vertical drop. The only beginner slope besides the bunny hill is a short run in a bowl at the top of the first gondola. The terrain is dominated by steep, advanced-level pistes.
offPisteDescription: Freeride routes start from Sella Golovec, at the left of the Funifor Slovita arrival. The area between Poviz, Leupa, Cergnala and the arrival of the old Canin cable car is considered a giant amusement park for freeride lovers. The resort receives heavy snowfall (over 13 meters in 2009) due to northern cold air meeting humid southern air, providing deep powder conditions for off-piste skiing.
valueDescription: Lift tickets are affordable: a 1-day adult pass costs €44, a youth pass €10, and a child pass €10. A 6-day pass is €189.50, and a season pass is €550. Ski and snowboard rental is reasonable, and food and drink at the resort are inexpensive. The resort is described as surprisingly affordable compared to other European resorts.
familyDescription: The resort is not well suited for families with beginners. The main runs from the gondola back to the base are steep, and the only beginner slope besides the bunny hill is a short run in a bowl at the top of the first gondola. A review noted having to carry a child's skis down the steepest part. There is one ski school, but the terrain is predominantly advanced and expert, with limited easy runs.
apresSkiDescription: The village has 2 bars/pubs and 1 club. Après-ski activities include ice skating and an indoor swimming pool. There are 2-3 restaurants in the village, and several lodges next to the slopes offer snacks and hot drinks. The scene is quiet and low-key, with a mix of Italian and Slovenian influences.
resortCharacterDescription: Sella Nevea is a small mountain pass village at 1,195 m, part of the Chiusaforte municipality. The ski resort started development in 1965 but suffered setbacks from avalanches in 1975 and bankruptcy in 1980. It is now a quiet, out-of-the-way resort with a historic inn (Refugio Julia) and a relaxed atmosphere. The village is not purpose-built but has a traditional alpine character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: 2 gondolas (including a modern gondola connecting to Bovec), 1 double chairlift, and 1 surface lift. The connection to Bovec uses a modern gondola over Sella Prevala to Sella Golovec, then a chairlift and slope to Bovec. Total lift capacity is 11,670 persons per hour. The longest gondola lift in Slovenia leads from Bovec to 2,202 m.
linkedResortsDescription: Since 2009, Sella Nevea is connected to the Bovec Kanin ski resort in Slovenia via a modern gondola lift over Sella Prevala to Sella Golovec, followed by a chairlift and slope to Bovec. A single ski pass covers both sides, creating a combined ski area of 30 km of slopes. The connection allows skiers to cross the border freely within the Schengen zone.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 14 km of slopes on 30 hectares, with a mix of blue, red, and black runs. The main attraction is the FIS World Cup Panorama slope, which hosts women's slalom and giant slalom races. A freeride area on the east slope provides powder runs and a fun run with rollers, jumps, and banked turns. 13 km of slopes are floodlit for night skiing, and the slopes are groomed twice daily.
offPisteDescription: The designated freeride area on the east slope offers powder slopes and a fun run with rollers, jumps, and banked turns, suitable for advanced skiers and snowboarders. No other off-piste terrain is described in the source text.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs €56.50, with discounts for youth (€51) and children (€28.50). Multi-day passes are available, e.g., a 6-day adult pass for €280.50. The Hirschi Family Discount allows the third child to ski free when parents and first two children purchase passes. The resort accepts the NÖ Bergerlebnispass and Ostalpenkarten. Night skiing tickets are €48.50 for adults.
familyDescription: The resort has a blue beginner slope and a separate beginners' area at the Happylift (independent operator). Two ski schools (Skischule Semmering and Schneesportschule Zauberberg) offer group and private lessons for children and adults. A 3 km illuminated toboggan run is available. The Hirschi Family Discount allows the third child to ski free. The resort is about an hour from Vienna, making it convenient for family day trips.
resortCharacterDescription: Semmering is a small town (population ~544) at the Semmering Pass, with a history as a summer resort for wealthy Viennese in the late 19th century. The town features historic hotels such as the Südbahnhotel (built 1882) and Hotel Panhans (1888). The ski area is on Hirschenkogel mountain, accessed from the pass. The resort is not purpose-built but a traditional village with a mix of historic and modern infrastructure.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a modern 8-person gondola and a 4-person chairlift, both operating during day and night. A separate tow lift (Happylift) serves a beginner slope. The gondola is also used by pedestrians and tobogganers. No information on lift age or queue times is provided.
linkedResortsDescription: The Semmering Hirschenkogel ski area is linked with the Stuhleck ski resort via ticket acceptance: day and multi-day passes from Stuhleck are valid at Semmering, and the Ostalpenkarte and Regional Karte Stuhleck are accepted. However, Semmering passes are not valid at Stuhleck. No physical lift connection is described.

terrainDescription: Sestriere's local terrain is divided into three main sectors: Sises in front of the village, Fraiteve behind it, and Banchetta to the east. The slopes include wide, sun-drenched cruisers on the Fraiteve side, such as the long top-to-bottom blue piste 26, and steeper challenges on the Banchetta side, including the Olympic Kandahar Banchetta Giovanni Nasi black run, which drops 1,000 vertical metres from 2,800m to 1,800m with a maximum gradient over 60%. The resort has 12 black slopes, 30 red runs, and 24 beginner slopes, with dedicated baby slopes at the foot of the Kandahar Giovanni Alberto Agnelli run, set in a natural amphitheatre. The Pattemouche red run descends through forest toward Pragelato, while the Sises blue piste offers gentle terrain for improving skiers. The local ski area covers 80–114 km of marked pistes, with 82 marked runs in total.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Sestriere is limited but rewarding with a guide. The north-facing slopes off Monte Fraiteve hold powder longest after a storm, as most visitors ski the sunnier aspects first. The less-tracked terrain between pistes offers powder hunting opportunities after fresh snowfall, particularly in the quieter corners of the Via Lattea. The resort's high altitude (village at 2,035m) extends the touring season, and the backcountry terrain around Mount Motta and Banchetta provides additional routes. However, the off-piste is not extensive compared to dedicated freeride resorts, and avalanche risk requires local knowledge.
valueDescription: Sestriere offers good value compared to many French resorts, with lift pass prices in the 2025/26 season ranging from €53 (low season adult 1-day) to €58.50 (high season adult 1-day), and a 6-day pass from €268 low season to €307 high season. Midweek stays provide excellent value as the resort quietens down, and early December and late March fall outside peak pricing windows. Accommodation is predominantly ski-in/ski-out, with many apartments and hotels offering convenient slope access. Mountain restaurants are reasonably priced, with Italian cuisine like pizza and pasta available at affordable rates. The resort's connection to the 400km Via Lattea area adds terrain value without a significant premium.
familyDescription: Sestriere is well-suited for families, with dedicated baby slopes at the foot of the Kandahar Giovanni Alberto Agnelli run, set in a natural amphitheatre where parents can watch from close by. The Anfiteatro area offers gentle, contained slopes perfect for young children, with a warming hut nearby. Ski schools run children's programmes from age four, with small group sizes and English-speaking instructors available. The compact village is easy to navigate on foot, and many accommodations are ski-in/ski-out. Off-slope activities include ice skating, dog sledding, snowmobiles, a sports centre, and a swimming pool. Childcare is provided by private services and selected hotels rather than a single resort-run centre.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Sestriere ranges from relaxed to lively. Energetic venues include Pinky bar and Tabata Disco, while GSK is known for a wild youth scene. The Igloo offers all-day food, entertainment, and good beer choices, and Gargote Fashion Café is popular for banging après-ski. However, the overall scene is more chilled than in nearby Sauze d'Oulx, with limited live music early in the season. Slope-side bars at the base of lifts provide convenient post-ski drinks. Dining options include traditional Alpine flavours at Chalet Raggio del Sole and contemporary Italian at I.Gloo Sestriere, as well as pizza and pasta at numerous mountain restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Sestriere is a purpose-built ski resort, developed in the 1930s by the Agnelli family (of Fiat fame) as one of the world's first such resorts. The village is dominated by two iconic hotel towers, designed by Vittorio Bonadè Bottino, which were the first buildings in the village and later served as part of the Olympic Village for the 2006 Winter Olympics. The resort is practical and convenient, with most accommodation offering ski-in/ski-out access, but it lacks traditional alpine charm. The architecture is functional, with concrete apartment blocks and modern facilities. The village centre is compact, with services and lifts within easy walking distance.
liftSystemDescription: Sestriere has 19–20 local ski lifts, including high-speed quads and gondolas, serving terrain from 1,357m to 2,840m. The lift system was modernised for the 2006 Winter Olympics, but some older chairlifts remain without covers or heating, leading to slow rides and potential bottlenecks at peak times. The resort has 69% of pistes covered by artificial snowmaking, and night skiing is available on a floodlit run. The wider Via Lattea area adds 70 lifts total, connecting to neighbouring resorts. Lift queues can be long during weekends and school holidays, particularly when high winds close the links to Sauze d'Oulx.
linkedResortsDescription: Sestriere is the central hub of the Via Lattea (Milky Way) ski area, which connects six resorts: Pragelato, Claviere, Sauze d'Oulx, Cesana Torinese/Sansicario, and Montgenèvre in France. The linked area offers over 400 km of skiable pistes served by 70 lifts, with a single ski pass. The connection to Montgenèvre allows skiing across the French border. The Pattemouche-Anfiteatro cableway, built in 2006, links Sestriere to Pragelato. The Via Lattea also includes the resorts of Sauze d'Oulx (known for lively après-ski), Sansicario (family-friendly), and Claviere (small and quiet). The highest point in the area is Monte Motta at 2,800m, and the lowest lift base is at 1,372m in Cesana Torinese.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 28 km of groomed pistes, with 68% easy (blue) and 25% intermediate (red) runs, plus 12 km of ski routes. Most runs are wide, scenic forest trails under the tree line. The longest run is the 5.5 km valley run to Flachauwinkl with a 980 m vertical drop. A 19 km Family Run crosses the entire area with 19 themed stations. A permanent speed skiing track is located on the Powder Shuttle slope. The resort also has a snow park (Absolut Park) with over 100 obstacles, a halfpipe, and separate areas for beginners, advanced, and pros.
offPisteDescription: Ski routes are spread throughout the ski area, with the Freeride Trail beneath the Powder Shuttle particularly recommended. There are several natural freeride routes, and powder zones exist in steeper sections and off-piste trails. The resort has 12 km of marked ski routes, and advanced riders can find steeper sections and fun off-piste trails in the trees.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €79 (2025/26 season), with youth at €59.50 and children at €39.50. Six-day passes are available for €370.50. The resort is part of the Ski amadé network, so multi-day passes grant access to 760 km of slopes across 5 top ski areas. Free ski buses run at short intervals between Flachauwinkl and Kleinarl. The resort has its own motorway exit (A10 Tauernautobahn), reducing transfer costs.
familyDescription: The resort is very well suited for families. At both valley stations, separate practice areas with magic carpets, snow carousels, and wave tracks are available. In Kleinarl, Pingo's Winter World offers a magic carpet, wave track, snow carousel, playground, and full-day supervision. The Flora Meadow practice area has two magic carpets and a snow carousel. The Family Run is a 19 km fun trail with 19 colorful Crazy Shuttleberg Dolls stations, a scavenger hunt, and weekly events. The Sun House at the Family Shuttle valley station features a drawing corner, comic cinema, sun terrace, and boot drying room. Child-friendly chairlifts with flat exits make it easy for youngsters to load.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Flachauwinkl and Kleinarl is friendly and laid-back, focused on relaxing after skiing. The Chill House at the bottom of the Absolut Park is a popular meeting place for snowboarders and freeskiers, offering music, videos, bouldering, and a mini ramp. Traditional mountain huts like Gasthof Walchau, Frauenalm, Hubertusalm, and Winklalm serve regional food and drinks. The Lumberjack restaurant at the bottom of the Lumberjack Shuttle is a food hall with organic meals and food trucks.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort consists of two small traditional villages: Kleinarl (population 792, elevation 1,014 m) and Flachauwinkl (elevation 989 m). Kleinarl has a Gothic St. Lawrence parish church consecrated in 1443 and a former hunting lodge of the Prince-Archbishops of Salzburg. The ski area itself is modern, with chairlifts and gondolas, and is accessed directly from the A10 Tauernautobahn motorway exit in Flachauwinkl. The villages retain an alpine character with traditional architecture.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 9 lifts, including modern chairlifts and gondolas. All chairlifts have flat exits suitable for children. The Panorama Link, a ten-seater gondola opened in December 2020, connects the resort to Wagrain and Zauchensee in nine minutes. Each lift serves at least two well-groomed runs. The resort has a capacity of 20,400 persons per hour. Lifts operate from 08:45 to 16:00.
linkedResortsDescription: The Shuttleberg Flachauwinkl-Kleinarl is connected via the Panorama Link gondola (opened December 2020) to the Snow Space Salzburg ski area (Flachau, Wagrain, St. Johann-Alpendorf) and the Zauchensee-Flachauwinkl ski area, creating a total of 210 km of interconnected pistes. It is also part of the Ski amadé network, Austria's largest ski association, which includes 5 top ski areas (Salzburger Sportwelt, Schladming-Dachstein, Gastein, Hochkönig, Grossarltal) with 270 lifts and 760 km of groomed slopes accessible on one ski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area is on the Thurntaler mountain, with a vertical drop of about 1,300 m from the summit at 2,407 m to the valley at 1,100 m. The main runs are long, groomed trails, including a 3,400-foot vertical descent from the top of the two-stage gondola back to the base. The upper mountain features wide, open snowfields between trails, and the terrain is predominantly intermediate, with a sunny southeast-facing aspect. Two valley runs lead down to Sillian and Außervillgraten. A snowpark with a 130 m wave track, 240 m ski cross, and various obstacles is located at 2,200 m on a southeast-facing slope.
offPisteDescription: Open snowfields between the groomed trails offer accessible off-piste skiing, with good snow cover in the upper areas. For more adventurous terrain, guided routes are recommended, particularly to the Hochalm and other unmarked ski routes. The ski area is known for its freeride terrain, and the sunny aspect means conditions can soften quickly, so early starts are advisable.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced at €64–70 for a day ticket for adults, with children (6–18 years) receiving a 50% discount and children under 6 skiing free. The Ski)Hit tariff offers further family discounts: for large families, only two adults and the two oldest children (up to 17.9 years) pay, while all additional children ski for free. The resort is noted for being more affordable than many better-known Alpine destinations, with a six-day adult pass at €362 and a child six-day pass at €181.
familyDescription: The resort is certified as family-friendly by the Tirol Tourist Board. The Bobo Club Hochpustertal provides a dedicated children's area with a 40 m conveyor belt, igloo, tunnel, carousel, wave run, and special children's lifts. The ski school offers child-oriented lessons for ages 3.5 and up, with a weekly final race and medals. Childcare is available for children from 24 months, and the Dolomiten Residenz Sporthotel offers professional childcare through the 'Häppi Päpi' program. The nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic, and the village is compact and walkable.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is quiet and relaxed, with a few bars and restaurants in the village. The Acquarena wellness centre, opened in 2018, offers thermal pools, saunas, and a spa. Mountain huts such as the Pistentreff serve drinks and food. There is no disco or large nightlife venue; the atmosphere is focused on family and outdoor activities.
resortCharacterDescription: Sillian is a traditional Tyrolean market town at 1,100 m, with preserved architecture featuring carved wooden balconies and decorated facades. The old town centre retains its historic charm, and the village is surrounded by the Lienz Dolomites and the Carnic Alps. It is not a purpose-built resort; the infrastructure blends modern amenities with alpine character. The village has about 2,100 inhabitants and 1,200 guest beds.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: 2 cable cars (gondolas), 1 six-seater chairlift with a weather protection hood and seat heating, 2 four-seater chairlifts, 1 T-bar, and 1 ski school lift. The main gondola from Sillian is a two-stage lift ascending 1,000 m vertical. The six-pack chairlift serves the upper mountain. The total lift capacity is 14,390 persons per hour. Snowmaking covers 95% of the pistes, ensuring reliable conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: The Sillian-Hochpustertal ski area is a single connected mountain with two access points: from Sillian and from Ausservillgraten. The slopes on the Thurntaler are served by lifts from both sides, and the valley runs descend to both villages. The Ski)Hit pass provides access to other East Tyrol ski areas, including Großglockner Resort Kals-Matrei, St. Jakob im Defereggental, and the Mölltaler Glacier, though these are not physically linked by lifts.

terrainDescription: Most runs in the Silvretta Arena are above the treeline in high alpine terrain, with wide and long slopes that are generally well connected between Ischgl and Samnaun. The valley runs down to Ischgl lead through forest clearings. The longest continuous descent is the Eleven run from Greitspitze to Ischgl Dorf, covering 11 km with 1,472 m of vertical drop. The steepest marked slope is 14a Lange Wand, 1.8 km long with a 633 m vertical drop and a gradient exceeding 70%. The ski area includes several ski routes (ungroomed marked itineraries) at Palinkopf, Höllspitze, Greitspitze, and Viderjoch, totalling 15 km. The Schmugglerrunde circuits (Gold, Silver, Bronze) offer long ski tours across the border, with the Gold circuit being one of the longest in the world at 35.7 km of slopes and 6,463 m of elevation difference.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available on designated freeride zones and marked ski routes. The Piz Val Gronda area offers 100 hectares of terrain specifically designated for freeriding. There are seven marked freeride routes ranging from moderate to extremely challenging, all requiring avalanche safety equipment (transceiver, probe, shovel). Ski patrol monitors these routes and closes them when avalanche danger is high. Guided backcountry tours are available to areas such as Piz Buin and the Jamtal Valley. The resort also has 15 km of ski routes (ungroomed itineraries) that provide off-piste experiences without full backcountry commitment.
valueDescription: For the 2025/26 season, a 6-day adult lift pass costs €361, with youth (15-18) at €289 and children (8-14) at €216.50. Children under 8 ski free when accompanied by a paying adult. The VIP Samnaun-Ischgl pass, available to guest card holders, offers reduced rates (6-day adult €359, children €215, free up to age 10). Early bird season passes purchased before October receive a 15-20% discount. The Silvretta Card bundles lift access with equipment rental, group lessons, and lunch vouchers starting at €410 for 6 days. Staying in nearby villages like Mathon or using the free ski bus to Galtür, Kappl, and See can reduce accommodation costs while still accessing the main ski area.
familyDescription: Ischgl and Samnaun offer dedicated children's areas. The Kinderland Idalp in Ischgl features a spacious children's area with several conveyor belts (one covered), a children's carousel, and fun figures. A children's restaurant and guest kindergarten are located at the Restaurant Idalp, where ski schools bring children for lunch. The Funline Ischgl, parallel to the Snowpark, offers waves, banked turns, speed boosters, and jumps suitable for families. In Samnaun, the Kinderland Winterblumentraum on the Musellahang has two covered conveyor belts, and the 1st Swiss Ski and Snowboard School Samnaun offers free hotel pickup and drop-off for children. A smaller children's area with conveyor belt and platter lift exists on Alp Trida, plus a playroom at Alp Trider Sattel. The child-friendly Sonnenbahn provides easy access to gentle slopes on the Idalp. Children under 8 ski free (or under 10 with the VIP pass). The resort mascot is Hugo, die grüne Panzerechse.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Ischgl starts on the mountain at the Paznauner Taja hut, a popular slope-side spot. Back in the village, the main après-ski circuit includes Trofana Alm and Kuhstall, both known for loud music and large crowds. For late-night clubbing, Fire & Ice stays open until dawn. The resort hosts three 'Top of the Mountain' concerts each season (opening, mid-season, and closing) featuring international stars such as Elton John, Robbie Williams, Rihanna, Christina Aguilera, and Rita Ora. Ischgl enforces a no-ski-boots rule in the streets after 8:00 PM to manage rowdy behaviour, so visitors should carry trainers. The village also has duty-free shopping in Samnaun, cigar lounges, and high-end restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Ischgl is a modern, purpose-built resort with a compact village centre that is pedestrian-friendly. The village features a 150-metre moving walkway tunnel (Dorftunnel) connecting the eastern and western parts, making it easy to traverse with gear. The main street is lined with hotels, shops, bars, and restaurants, creating a lively and polished atmosphere. The architecture is a blend of traditional Tyrolean style and modern development, but the resort is unapologetically lively and focused on entertainment, with high-end shopping and gourmet dining. It is not a sleepy, traditional alpine village; rather, it is designed for convenience and social activity, with lift access directly from the village centre.
liftSystemDescription: The Silvretta Arena has 45 modern lifts with a total capacity of around 97,000 people per hour. Notable lifts include the world's first double-decker cable car (from Samnaun), the 3S Pardatschgratbahn (a three-cable gondola from Ischgl to 2,600 m), and high-speed detachable chairlifts such as the 8-passenger Greitspitzbahn and 6-passenger Palinkopfbahn with heated seats and weather bubbles. Three main gondolas (Silvrettabahn, Fimbabahn, Pardatschgratbahn) transport skiers from the village to the Idalp mid-station in about 10-12 minutes. Queues are rare even in peak season due to high capacity. The resort also features the Ischgl Skyfly, a 2 km zipline from the Silvrettabahn mid-station reaching speeds of up to 85 km/h.
linkedResortsDescription: The Silvretta Arena is lift-linked with the Swiss resort of Samnaun, creating a cross-border ski area with multiple crossing points. The Silvretta ski pass also grants access to the nearby resorts of Galtür (Silvapark Galtür, 43 km of slopes), Kappl (over 40 km), and See (over 40 km) via a free ski bus service that runs every 30 minutes. This expands the total skiable terrain to over 366 km. The bus from Ischgl to Galtür takes about 20 minutes, to Kappl about 15 minutes, and to See about 17 minutes.

terrainDescription: Ski amadé's terrain is spread across 25 ski areas in five regions: Salzburger Sportwelt, Schladming-Dachstein, Gastein, Hochkönig, and Grossarltal. The slopes are predominantly wide, well-groomed runs suitable for beginners and intermediates, with 10 snowparks and 13 fun slopes adding variety. The highest lift reaches 2,700 m on the Dachstein Glacier, and the network is linked by free ski buses between resorts. The character is distinctly Austrian, with friendly villages and lively hut culture.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in several areas, particularly Sportgastein, which offers deep snow runs at high altitude (up to 2,686 m). The tree skiing in the Gastein Valley and north-facing bowls above Zauchensee hold powder well after storms. However, off-piste opportunities are more limited than in higher-altitude French or Swiss resorts, and local knowledge is recommended for safe exploration.
valueDescription: Ski amadé offers a range of value options with a single pass covering all 760 km. Discounted rates are available for children, teens, and seniors, and family discounts apply. Local valley passes are cheaper for those staying in one area. Special packages like the 4=3 Online Package and Learn2Ski in 3 days provide additional savings. Accommodation ranges from budget guesthouses to luxury hotels, with deals such as 7 nights half board in a 4-star hotel including a 6-day pass from €1,104 per person.
familyDescription: Ski amadé is well-suited for families, with dedicated nursery areas in Filzmoos, Werfenweng, and Bad Hofgastein that are separated from faster traffic. Many resorts have magic carpets and easy greens. Grossarltal offers a free adventure meadow with a lift and magic carpet, plus a kids' snowpark. Ski schools are widely available with English-speaking instructors. The villages are family-friendly, with car-free options like Werfenweng and a relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Ski amadé is more about relaxed hut culture and good food than wild parties. The region has hundreds of mountain huts serving local cuisine. Schladming has a lively town centre with bars, and Bad Gastein offers the Silver Bullet bar. However, the overall atmosphere is low-key, with most visitors focusing on skiing rather than nightlife. The region is known for its friendly, traditional Austrian hospitality.
resortCharacterDescription: Ski amadé is a network of 25 traditional Austrian villages and mid-sized resorts, each with its own character. Resorts range from the historic spa town of Bad Gastein with Belle Époque architecture to the lively town of Schladming with a pedestrianised centre. The villages are generally picturesque, with alpine charm, and the network is linked by buses. The character is distinctly Austrian, with friendly villages and well-groomed slopes.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Ski amadé comprises modern lifts, many with heated seats and pull-down covers. The network is designed for efficient circulation between sectors, with high-capacity gondolas and chairlifts. Queues are rare outside peak holiday weeks. The infrastructure is well-maintained, with significant annual investment in new lifts and upgrades.
linkedResortsDescription: Ski amadé is a network of 25 ski areas across five regions (Salzburger Sportwelt, Schladming-Dachstein, Gastein, Hochkönig, and Grossarltal) linked by a single ski pass and bus connections. The resorts are not continuously connected by lifts but are accessible via free ski buses. The pass also includes free entry to thermal baths and other benefits. The network covers 760 km of pistes and 270 lifts, offering a wide variety of terrain across traditional Austrian villages.

terrainDescription: The ski area has two distinct sides. The north-facing slopes above Moena feature mainly red and black runs, including the steep Piavac descent with a notorious initial drop and the Canalone Tomba slope used by Alberto Tomba for training. The south-facing area towards Bellamonte is sunny, wide and gentle, with blue and red runs such as the Zirmes run offering views of the Pale di San Martino. The south side is described as a realm for families and first-timers.
offPisteDescription: The Col Margherita area (2,513 m) includes a Freeride Park for challenging off-piste terrain. The north face of Alpe Lusia, with its steep black runs like Piavac and Canalone Tomba, also offers natural off-piste opportunities for experienced skiers. A snowpark and boardercross track are available for freestyle enthusiasts.
valueDescription: Daily lift passes start at around €50 for adults and €35 for children. A 6-day Tre Valli ski pass costs €254 for adults in low season and €197 for children. The resort is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which offers a single pass for 1,200 km of slopes across 12 areas. Accommodation in nearby villages like Moena and Predazzo is reasonably priced, with self-catering apartments and family-run hotels available.
familyDescription: The south-facing Bellamonte side is specifically designed for families, with wide, sunny nursery slopes, a snow playground, and indoor heated areas for children. The Fraina sledding track (2.1 km) winds through the forest. Themed play areas Giro d'Ali and Frainus are located near the Bellamonte 3.0 gondola. Ski schools offer qualified children's instructors, and the village of Moena is pedestrian-friendly with a relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: The resort offers cosy mountain refuges and restaurants, with après-ski and lounge bars at the base areas. Moena hosts the Moena Vintage Ski Revival in late March, featuring non-competitive races on period gear and free concerts at mountain huts. The overall nightlife is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on dining and socialising in the village.
resortCharacterDescription: Moena, known as the 'Fairy of the Dolomites', is a traditional alpine town with historic streets, stone fountains, and wooden barns (tabià) decorated with floral arrangements. It is part of the Alpine Pearls network for sustainable tourism. The ski area itself is not a single purpose-built resort but a collection of villages (Moena, Bellamonte, Passo San Pellegrino) set within the UNESCO-listed Dolomites.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of modern gondolas and covered chairlifts. Key lifts include the Ronchi-Valbona 8-person gondola, Valbona-Le Cune 8-person gondola, Piavac 4-person chairlift with canopy, Campo-Le Cune 4-person chairlift with canopy, Campo-Lastè 4-person chairlift with canopy, Castelir-Fassane 10-person gondola, and Fassane-Morea 10-person gondola. The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:15 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Alpe Lusia is part of the Alpe Lusia/San Pellegrino ski area, which includes Moena, Bellamonte, Passo San Pellegrino, and Falcade, offering 100 km of slopes. A free ski shuttle (Skitour Tre Valli) connects Alpe Lusia with San Pellegrino. The resort is also part of the Dolomiti Superski network, granting access to 1,200 km of pistes across 12 ski areas, including the Sellaronda circuit and links to Val di Fiemme/Obereggen via the Fiemme-Obereggen ski pass.

terrainDescription: The Valchiavenna Ski Area offers 34 slopes covering 40 km, ranging from blue to black, accessed from the village of Madesimo or via the Sky Express funicular from Campodolcino. Slopes are groomed with natural and artificial snow, and night skiing is available on the illuminated Pianello/Montalto slope. A snow park, MadePark, is located at 1800 m with kickers, boxes, and rails for freestyle skiing.
offPisteDescription: The Canalone is a renowned off-piste descent, considered one of the most challenging in the Alps, accessed from the top of the ski area. Additional off-piste itineraries are available in the Val di Lei area, though access depends on lift upgrades which are currently underway.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €44 for adults and €25 for children, with a six-day pass at €204. The resort offers promotional tickets like the 'Ridottodì' weekday ticket. Accommodation and dining are reasonably priced compared to larger Alpine resorts, with mountain huts serving traditional dishes at moderate prices.
familyDescription: A nursery slope and race track are located near the village, accessible on foot, providing a safe learning area for beginners and children. Ski schools offer lessons for adults and children, and the compact village layout means slopes start from the village, making it convenient for families.
resortCharacterDescription: Madesimo is a traditional alpine village in the Valchiavenna region, with stone-built houses and a historic centre. The resort has grown around the existing village, with slopes starting from the village itself. The village offers a relaxed atmosphere with local restaurants and shops.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 11 lifts, with a mix of chairlifts and cable cars. The Sky Express is an underground funicular connecting Campodolcino to Alpe Motta in 3 minutes, and the Larici cable car runs from Madesimo village to 1900 m. Lifts are modern with automatic coupling, though some lifts are currently being modernized, and the Val di Lei lift is closed pending upgrades.
linkedResortsDescription: The Valchiavenna Ski Area links the resorts of Madesimo and Campodolcino. The Sky Express funicular connects Campodolcino to Alpe Motta, which is part of the Madesimo ski area, allowing skiers to access the same slopes from both villages. The area is not part of a larger linked domain like Dolomiti Superski.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located on the northern slopes of Klínovec mountain (1,244 m) in the Krušné hory (Ore Mountains), with slopes running through a dense fir forest. The resort offers 32 pistes totaling 31.4 km of skiable terrain, with a vertical drop of 414 m. The longest run is 3.4 km, descending to the spa town of Jáchymov. Terrain is split 59% intermediate, 28% advanced, and 13% expert. The forest setting provides shelter from wind, and the slopes are groomed for carving, with some tree-lined sections allowing adventurous skiing after snowfall.
valueDescription: A day lift ticket costs €49 (approximately 1,200 CZK). On-slope beer is under €4. Free parking is available with over 2,000 spaces across multiple lots. Accommodation in the nearby UNESCO-listed spa town of Karlovy Vary (15 km away) is available for under €100 per night in high-quality hotels. Ski-in/ski-out lodging is limited and often fully booked. The resort offers online ski pass purchases at a discount, and multi-day passes include access to the German side via the InterSkiregion without extra cost.
familyDescription: The resort has dedicated children's areas including a baby ski park and five children's conveyor belts. A ski school offers lessons for beginners and advanced skiers and snowboarders of all ages, with English-speaking instructors available. There is a tubing lane and two terrain parks. Free parking and ski buses reduce logistics. However, visitor reviews note that on peak days (e.g., New Year's Eve) queues can exceed one hour, and the single cable car serving multiple slopes creates bottlenecks, making it less ideal for families with young children on busy weekends.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort has two main entrance points: Jáchymov (a historic radon spa town) and Loučná pod Klínovcem, both with free parking. The lift infrastructure mixes modern high-speed quad chairlifts (e.g., Prima Express, Suzuki) with older T-bar surface lifts, particularly in the Neklid section. The slopes are entirely within fir forest, not above treeline, offering protected skiing. The base area is purpose-built with ticket offices, ski rental, and a few restaurants, but lacks a traditional alpine village atmosphere. The nearby town of Karlovy Vary provides historic spa architecture and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 18 lifts: 3 high-speed quad chairlifts, 1 fixed-grip quad chair, 1 triple chair, and 13 surface lifts (T-bars and platters). There are also 5 children's conveyor belts. The main access from the base uses the Prima Express quad and the Suzuki quad. A free ski bus runs every 10 minutes between Klínovec and the German resort Fichtelberg, taking 15 minutes. Visitor reviews report that queues can be very long on weekends and holidays, with waits of up to 1.5 hours at popular lifts, and the single cable car (Suzuki) is often overcrowded.
linkedResortsDescription: Klínovec is linked with the German resort Fichtelberg via the InterSkiregion project. A multi-day ski pass (minimum 1.5 days) purchased at either resort is valid across both areas, giving access to 47 km of slopes in total. A free ski bus runs every 10 minutes between the resorts, taking 15 minutes. This allows skiers to cross the border and ski in two countries on the same ticket. The combined area includes 25 lifts and a variety of terrain on both sides of the mountain.

terrainDescription: The ski area features wide, clear slopes that are fully artificially snowed and lit. The longest run is 3.2 km. The terrain is predominantly gentle, with a mix of beginner, intermediate, and advanced runs. The slopes are well-maintained and offer good visibility.
valueDescription: The resort offers free parking at both base stations (Pstruží and near Abertamy). Lift passes are affordable compared to Alpine resorts, making it a budget-friendly option. Ski equipment rental and ski school are available on-site.
familyDescription: The resort has one of the largest children's ski parks in the Czech Republic, equipped with four moving carpet lifts (83m, 42m, 40m, 30m), a children's ski carousel, and snow-tubing. A ski school is available, and there are sledding tracks for families. The children's centre is located near the slopes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Apres Ski bar U Dostyho on the slope, the Skibar on another slope, and the mountain-top restaurant Plešivec with panoramic views. Additional dining is available at the bistro Stodola and restaurant Fabrika Art in the Zelené Údolí chalet.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a modern, purpose-built ski area in the Ore Mountains, with a summit at 1,028 m featuring a lookout tower, hotel, and restaurant. The base stations are at Pstruží and near Abertamy, with free parking. It is one of the newest ski resorts in the Czech Republic, using modern technology for snowmaking and lifts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three four-seater cable cars (including two express detachable chairs) and six surface lifts, for a total of nine lifts. The cable cars have a high transport capacity of up to 2,400 people per hour each, minimizing queues. The slopes are fully artificially snowed and lit.

terrainDescription: The ski area includes a race slope with time measurement, floodlit night skiing on 2 km, and a terrain park with three lines featuring boxes, jumps, rollers, and rails. The Hochgrat mountain (1,833 m) offers wide, generous trails ideal for carving, and the Imberg plateau is a sunny high-altitude area with good conditions for learning. Ski circuits are well-groomed throughout the resort, and snowmaking covers 12 km of pistes.
familyDescription: The Imberg top station has a ski kids park with a ski kindergarten and ski school, located on a sunny high-altitude plateau that provides good conditions for learning. The resort has a baby-lift and gentle slopes suitable for beginners, and the ski school offers English-speaking instructors.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is centered around the Bärenstadl at the top station and the umbrella bar at the base station of the Imberg gondola. There is one bar/pub in the resort.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 14 lifts including a gondola, a high-speed six-person chairlift, and surface lifts. A new chairlift was installed two years ago, and queues are generally minimal according to reviews.
linkedResortsDescription: The Skiarena Steibis is part of the larger Oberstaufen ski area, which also includes the slopes of Thalkirchdorf and Hochgrat, offering a combined 40 km of pistes and 28 lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski area stretches between Bertoldi and Passo Vezzena across the Alpe Cimbra plateau, with slopes winding through alpine coniferous forests and clearings. All parts are connected by easy runs, allowing beginners and children to explore the entire area. Specific named runs include the blue runs 'Le Stock', 'Vezzena', 'Torrion', 'Le Cogole', and 'Fratelle', the red 'Ust' run leading to Malga Laghetto, and the black 'Avez del Prinzep' run descending from Monte Cucco. A four-seater chairlift serves the summit of Monte Cucco, with descents to Passo Vezzena and Malga Rivetta. A snowpark is also available for freestyle enthusiasts.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for the main season costs €48 for adults, €36 for youth, and €26 for children when purchased at the resort. Online prices are slightly higher: €49 weekday / €55 weekend for adults, with similar proportional pricing for youth and children. A six-day pass is available for around €250. The Alpe Cimbra full-area pass, covering Lavarone, Folgaria, and Fiorentini, offers access to 104 km of slopes and is priced higher than the local Lavarone-only pass. Accommodation and dining in the area are noted for being reasonably priced compared to larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: Lavarone is widely considered one of the most beginner-friendly ski areas in Trentino, with four dedicated beginner areas equipped with magic carpets at Lavarone Bertoldi and Passo Vezzena. The Lavarone Ski School employs over 50 instructors offering lessons in skiing and snowboarding. All slopes in the ski area are connected by easy runs, so families can ski together across the entire domain without encountering difficult terrain. The atmosphere is calm and uncrowded, with well-groomed pistes that are safe for children.
resortCharacterDescription: Lavarone is a small, peaceful ski resort set on the forested Alpe Cimbra plateau in Trentino. The landscape is characterised by alpine coniferous forests, clearings, and cozy wooden mountain huts (malghe) that serve as rest stops. The resort has a quiet, traditional atmosphere rather than a purpose-built feel, with the village of Lavarone itself offering a relaxed base. The ski area is compact and uncrowded, surrounded by gentle hills and scenic woodland.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area is served by 14 lifts: 6 chairlifts (including a four-seater chairlift to Monte Cucco), 2 T-bars, and 6 magic carpets. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with the entire area covered by automatic snowmaking. The lift network is compact and easy to navigate, with all parts of the ski area connected by easy slopes. A wider Alpe Cimbra pass also provides access to lifts in Folgaria and Fiorentini.
linkedResortsDescription: Lavarone is part of the Alpe Cimbra ski area, which also includes the Folgaria and Fiorentini ski zones. These are connected via the 'Skitour dei Forti' ski carousel, allowing skiers to explore a combined total of 104 km of slopes with a single Alpe Cimbra pass. The linked resorts offer additional terrain across the plateau, with bus connections between the sectors.

terrainDescription: The Skicircus offers 270 km of marked pistes across four interconnected valleys, with 156 runs (87 beginner, 55 intermediate, 14 advanced). The terrain is dominated by wide, well-groomed cruising runs that are ideal for intermediates, with the longest run being 7 km from Schattberg to Saalbach. The Skicircus Challenge is a 65 km circuit with 12,400 m of vertical descent that can be completed in a day. North-facing slopes above Hinterglemm hold snow better and are preferred for late-season skiing, while south-facing slopes on the north side of the valley are sunnier. Dedicated nursery areas exist at village level in Saalbach, Hinterglemm, and Leogang. Twelve marked black runs are scattered across the area, with the steepest sustained pitches on the Zwölferkogel and Schattberg sectors.
offPisteDescription: Fieberbrunn is a freeride hotspot and has been part of the Freeride World Tour since 2013, with the tour returning in early March 2026. The Wildseeloder sector offers lift-accessed lines that hold powder well after storms, and guided itineraries are available through local companies such as S4 and Widman, both based at the Fieberbrunn Bergbahn. The north-facing terrain above Fieberbrunn provides accessible backcountry with steep faces and couloirs. Off-piste is extensive across the Skicircus, with bowls, tree runs, and guided tours available for those with proper equipment. The area is known for thigh-deep untouched powder only metres from pistes on the north side of the valley.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs approximately €330 (€66/day), with children aged 6–15 receiving a 50% reduction and under-6s skiing free. Family passes and beginner passes for local lifts are available at reduced rates. Accommodation ranges from budget guesthouses and self-catered apartments in Fieberbrunn and Leogang to mid-range chalet-hotels and luxury spa hotels, with nightly rates from around €1,200 to €3,300 per room. The resort is noted for being cheaper than Swiss counterparts, and the free ski bus between villages reduces transport costs. Mountain dining is reasonably priced, with traditional Austrian fare available at over 60 huts.
familyDescription: Dedicated nursery slopes sit at village level in Saalbach, Hinterglemm, and Leogang, providing safe learning areas separate from faster traffic. Ski schools in both Saalbach and Hinterglemm offer English-language instruction for children from age four. The wide, well-groomed blue and red runs provide ideal progression terrain, and the modern lift system with heated seats and covers makes it easy for families to get around. The free ski bus connects all villages every ten minutes, and many hotels offer ski-in/ski-out access. The resort scores 4.58/5 for family friendliness, with user reviews noting 'lots of gentle runs for children' and 'very family-friendly'.
apresSkiDescription: Saalbach's apres-ski scene is among the liveliest in Austria, starting mid-afternoon on-mountain at Hinterhag Alm with live music, then migrating to Bauer's Schi-Alm at the base and into village bars such as Bobby's Pub, Castello, and Goaßstall. The atmosphere is energetic but less intense than St. Anton, described as 'oompah-pop' and 'very lively' with 'lots of options'. Hinterglemm is quieter, while Saalbach has more late-night venues. The scene is known for being fun without the premium price tag of some other Austrian resorts. Traditional drinks include Bombardino, Hinterglemmer (black tea with rum and schnaps), and Lumumba (hot chocolate with rum).
resortCharacterDescription: Saalbach and Hinterglemm are traditional Austrian villages with pedestrian zones, compact centres, and direct slope access. Saalbach is the livelier hub with most restaurants, bars, and shops, drawing a younger, social crowd. Hinterglemm is quieter and more spread out, suiting families and wellness seekers. Leogang is a sophisticated retreat with family-run spa resorts and a focus on design and sustainability. Fieberbrunn offers authentic Austrian charm with a relaxed atmosphere and is known as a freeride destination. The area is marketed as 'Home of Lässig' (home of laid-back style) and combines traditional alpine architecture with modern infrastructure.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 70 lifts: 29 gondolas/cable cars, 21 chairlifts, and 20 drag lifts, with most featuring modern amenities such as heated seats and retractable weather covers. High-speed six- and eight-person chairlifts dominate, and new 10-seater gondolas (12er NORD and Streubödenbahn) have been added, reducing waiting times. The system is noted for its efficiency, with user reviews rating lift efficiency 4.84/5 and calling it 'the best in Europe' and 'unbeatable'. Ski bridges and underpasses improve safety and flow. Snowmaking covers over 90% of pistes, ensuring reliable conditions even at lower altitudes. Queues are rare outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: The Skicircus connects four villages—Saalbach, Hinterglemm, Leogang, and Fieberbrunn—into one of Austria's largest linked ski areas, with 270 km of pistes on a single lift pass. The TirolS gondola links Hinterglemm with Fieberbrunn, completing the full circuit. The area is also part of the Alpin Card, which includes Zell am See–Kaprun, and is included in the Epic Pass. The Skicircus Challenge is a 65 km circuit that tours the entire area in one day. The connection to Fieberbrunn, added in 2015, brought freeride terrain into the network.

terrainDescription: The ski area covers 72km of pistes across 52 runs, with 56% graded red and 8% black, dropping through open alpine meadows and dense forest beneath Monte Civetta's 3,220m rock face; the terrain is linked village-to-village from Alleghe through Selva di Cadore to Val di Zoldo, with the north-facing Val di Zoldo sector holding cold snow and firm conditions into the afternoon after storms, while the Alleghe-facing slopes soften earlier; Gran Zuita descends 1,100m from Col Fioret (2,082m) to Alleghe Lake (1,000m) across three linked slopes with 360-degree Dolomite panoramas including views of the Sella Group, Marmolada, and Croda da Lago.
offPisteDescription: Forest terrain between marked runs holds snow well after storms and provides accessible tree skiing, with the north-facing Pelmo flanks offering powder stashes; above the treeline, steeper bowls and gullies reward exploration when conditions are right, while Monte Civetta's flanks present challenging touring routes for experienced mountaineers; the Marmolada glacier and surrounding Sella and Tofana groups provide high-alpine terrain accessible on the Dolomiti Superski pass, with the Marmolada summit descent being one of the most celebrated in the range; freeriding is prohibited in the resort itself and a guide is recommended for all off-piste exploration.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost €67 for adults (€62-67 depending on pre-purchase), with children paying €22-47 and seniors €47-61; food and drink prices are described as very reasonable, with Rifugio Palafavera noted for plentiful, cheap, and delicious cuisine; accommodation is mostly family-run hotels and holiday flats that are budget-friendly; night skiing from the Pecol gondola provides additional value; however, parking fees apply in Alleghe.
familyDescription: Nursery slopes with surface lifts exist at each base village, with Palafavera's being the gentlest and most sheltered, though there are no dedicated green-graded runs; the ski area is described as ideal for families with gentle progression terrain for returning beginners, quiet slopes with less pressure from faster skiers, and a family-friendly snowpark at Col dei Baldi (1,470m) with kickers, rails, and boxes for children and beginners; Alleghe has a population of around 1,100, offering a minimarket, several hotels, and an ice rink, with ski buses connecting to other areas twice hourly.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is described as quite relaxed with a rating of 3 out of 5 stars; the only true umbrella bar is Fontanabona, located directly on the Coldai slope at 1,497m, requiring departure before 4:30pm to catch the last lift to the valley; additional bars include Bar at Crep di Pecol and Baita Ru on the valley run to Pecol, with cozy atmosphere at valley stations to end the ski day; evening options are limited with no party bars, though La Botte and Bar Pizzeria Al Sole offer local cuisine and nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Alleghe is a traditional mountain village of around 1,100 residents sitting on the shore of Lago di Alleghe, an alpine lake created by a landslide in 1771, with Monte Civetta's thousand-metre rock face reflected in the water; Selva di Cadore is a collective of Ladin-speaking villages in the Val Fiorentina preserving the ancient Dolomite language and Veneto culinary traditions; Val di Zoldo remains less developed for tourism than other UNESCO Dolomites areas, offering authentic historically-rich villages; the overall feel is described as a place tourism hasn't yet reshaped, with the quieter and more authentic side of the Dolomites compared to the more developed South Tyrolean resorts to the north.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 23 lifts including 3 gondolas and 10 chairlifts with 10 surface drags, with six high-speed quads on the main sectors; lift capacity is 34,010 people per hour with opening hours 08:30-16:30; the Alleghe-Civetta gondola and Zoldo Valley lifts service the main verticals; the system links Alleghe, Selva di Cadore, Palafavera, and Val di Zoldo, enabling village-to-village circuit skiing; 98% of slopes are covered by a modern snowmaking system with 285 snow cannons and 14 snowcats maintaining conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: Civetta is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, one of 12 ski areas comprising 1,200km of pistes across the Dolomites; the ski area itself links Alleghe, Selva di Cadore, Palafavera, and Val di Zoldo with ski bus connections to Marmolada glacier, Cortina d'Ampezzo, and the famous Sellaronda ski circuit (accessible by car in about an hour); the area is a major link in the Grande Guerra (Great War) ski tour, a First World War history route; day trips to Arabba, Alta Badia, and Val Gardena are feasible on the Dolomiti Superski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 46 km of slopes, with 54% beginner (wide blue runs), 32% intermediate (red runs winding through forests and open alpine areas), and 14% advanced (including the Grubhörndl black run and Hochwena-Abfahrt). The Family Express chairlift serves the main learning zone, and the Lofi Funline provides a small snow park with waves, jumps, and banked turns. The longest run is 9 km. Snowmaking covers 36 km of slopes, and 90 snow machines with a 135,000 m³ water reservoir support snow reliability.
valueDescription: Day tickets cost €62 for adults, €49.50 for youth, and €31 for children. Family deals include a one-day ticket for 2 adults and 1 child at €143.50 in high season, and a 6-day family pass at €737.50. Children up to 18 ski free on Saturdays when accompanied by two paying parents. A season ticket valid across the 3 Länder Freizeit-Arena (covering Lofer, Steinplatte/Waidring, Winkelmoos, St. Johann in Tirol, Pillersee/Buchensteinwand, Unken/Heutal, Kirchdorf, and Erpfendorf) is available. Self-catering apartments and family-run hotels keep accommodation costs moderate.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family paradise. The children's area features a 140-meter-long covered conveyor belt for all-weather comfort. The Family Express chairlift serves gentle blue runs ideal for learning. Two child-safe 8-seater chairlifts are in operation. Ski schools offer English-speaking instructors. The village is compact and walkable, with a ski bus to the slopes. The main road is in a tunnel, keeping the village quiet. Accommodation includes family-run hotels and apartments.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and family-oriented, with hotel bars and local pubs such as the Dankl. Mountain huts like the Kechtalm serve traditional Austrian dishes. There is no chaotic nightlife; the scene is quiet and authentic.
resortCharacterDescription: Lofer is a traditional market town in the Saalach valley, with a historic parish church and typical Tyrolean architecture. The village is compact and quiet, as the main road bypasses it through a tunnel. Accommodation is in family-run hotels and apartments. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a working village character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts: 2 gondolas (Almbahn I and II), 2 eight-person chairlifts (including the SenderExpress and Family Express), 1 six-person chairlift, and 5 surface lifts. Three chairlifts have weather shields and heated seats. The gondolas are modern and efficient. The system is well-maintained and queues are rare outside peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is linked with the Heutal lifts in Unken, forming the 'Almenwelt Lofer & Heutal' region. A season ticket (3 Länder Freizeit-Arena) also covers Steinplatte/Waidring, Winkelmoos, St. Johann in Tirol, Pillersee/Buchensteinwand, Kirchdorf, and Erpfendorf. However, the main ski area is a small family resort with a connection to Unken.

terrainDescription: The ski area is situated between 896 m and 1,300 m, with slopes almost entirely covered by artificial snow. The terrain is dominated by easy blue runs, making up 64% of the 12.5 km of pistes, with intermediate reds at 20% and difficult blacks at 16%. The Hennesteck summit offers panoramic views and a mountain hut. For variety, the Anna-Park provides obstacles, kickers, a wave track, and a boardercross course, while the Anna-Land is a dedicated beginner and children's area with a fairytale forest. The Fis Abfahrt 1a is a noted black run for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: The NÖ Bergerlebnispass season pass covers 10 ski areas in Lower Austria, including Annaberg, with a super pre-sale price of €410 for adults and free for children when purchasing two adult passes. A single lift ticket also works across three linked ski mountains on the Mariazellerbahn line: Annaberg, Gemeindealpe Mitterbach, and Bürgeralpe, providing access to 50 km of runs. A free ski bus connects Bürgeralpe and Gemeindealpe. The resort is noted for being affordable, with a 6-day adult pass in the Dachstein West region (if applicable) around €170, though this may vary.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly popular with families. The Anna-Land children's area features a fairytale forest with speaking animals, a children's playground, and specially equipped beginner slopes. The largest ski school in Lower Austria offers lessons for children. Off-slope activities include tobogganing (floodlit on Saturdays), snowshoeing, winter hiking, cross-country skiing, and horse-drawn sleigh rides. The village is traffic-free in the centre, and the ski area is well-organised with short distances.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with two bars and pubs in the village and three restaurants. On the mountain, the Anna-Alm on the Hennesteck and the Eisbar offer refreshments, while valley options include the Teichstüberl and Reidlhütte. The scene is relaxed, with rustic huts and a cosy atmosphere rather than a rowdy nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Annaberg is a traditional alpine village in the Mostviertel region, with charming accommodations and rustic ski huts typical of the area. The resort offers views of the Ötscher mountain. It is not a purpose-built station but a genuine village with a cozy, family-friendly atmosphere. The village centre is walkable, and the ski area is directly accessible from the village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts, including 2 chairlifts, 2 drag lifts, and 2 T-bars, with a total capacity of 5,652 persons per hour. The lifts have been modernised in recent years and are described as having the latest technology. The main lift takes guests up to the Hennesteck summit at 1,300 m. Lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Annaberg is part of the Schiland Voralpen association, which links three ski mountains along the Mariazellerbahn rail line: Annaberg, Gemeindealpe Mitterbach, and Bürgeralpe. A single lift ticket is valid in all three areas, and a free ski bus runs between Bürgeralpe and Gemeindealpe. Additionally, the NÖ Bergerlebnispass season pass covers 10 ski areas in Lower Austria, including Annaberg, offering access to 51 lifts and 125 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area comprises 12 groomed runs totaling 17 km, with 6 km novice, 7 km intermediate, and 4 km expert terrain. Slopes are predominantly north-facing (65% of terrain), with a black run descending from the Hahnenwipfel peak offering a long north-facing descent. The resort's summit is at 1,600 m and base at 680 m, giving a vertical drop of 920 m. A fun park is available for freestylers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities are primarily ski touring and snowshoeing. Ski tourers can ascend 800 vertical meters from the valley to the summit, and free ski touring nights are held every Thursday from 5 p.m. on the valley run. Snowshoeing and winter hiking routes are available from the mountain station, with a new family adventure trail planned. No specific off-piste skiing quality is described.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €49 for adults, €36.50 for youths, and €24.50 for children (2025/26 season). Free parking is available directly in front of the mountain railways. The resort is smaller and less crowded than larger Carinthian destinations, offering a budget-friendly option for families and beginners.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family adventure mountain with a dedicated ski school (HAPPY Skischule Dreiländereck) offering professional courses and ski hire. An 8.4 km natural toboggan run, a panoramic ice skating rink at the mountain station, and a planned family adventure trail with interactive stations provide non-skiing activities. The slopes are predominantly easy (74% beginner) and intermediate (13%), with gentle terrain suitable for children.
apresSkiDescription: On-mountain apres-ski is limited to the Panorama Mountain Restaurant with a sun terrace serving Austrian specialties. The nearest lively apres-ski scene and nightclubs are in Villach, a 20-minute drive away. Some laid-back spots for hot drinks and meals are available in the base area and nearby villages.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, traditional mountain ski area on the Dreiländereck peak, located at the tripoint of Austria, Italy, and Slovenia. The base area at Seltschach has free parking and a few facilities, but no purpose-built village. The nearby town of Arnoldstein (2 km away) is a historic market town with a ruined abbey, offering accommodation and services. The resort has been operating since the 1930s and is popular for day trips from Villach and across the borders.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: one 3-seater chairlift and six T-bar surface lifts. Total uphill capacity is 8,100 persons per hour.
