However, there is a joint annual pass with the nearby Marbachegg ski area for summer operation, but no direct ski connection. The Schrattenfluh area offers ski touring but is not lift-served.

terrainDescription: The St. Anton sector of Ski Arlberg features steep, north-facing terrain on the Valluga and Schindlergrat ridges, with the highest piste skiing in the Arlberg at around 2,650m. The Rendl area (2,645m) offers west-facing slopes with a mix of blue, red, and black runs, including an all-blue circuit. Gampen (1,850m) and Kapall (2,330m) are predominantly south-facing with tree cover, providing nursery slopes at the base and a network of blue and red runs. The Galzig (2,185m) sector connects to St. Christoph and the Steissbach valley, with east-facing descents. The St. Anton-St. Christoph-Stuben sub-area contains approximately 70 marked runs and 30 lifts, with a slope distribution of roughly 34% blue, 37% red, and 29% black across the wider Ski Arlberg.
offPisteDescription: St. Anton is a renowned freeride destination with over 200 km of documented off-piste itineraries across the Arlberg. The Valluga summit (2,811m) is accessible only with a mountain guide for skiers, offering steep descents into the surrounding bowls. The Rendl sector includes extreme ski routes classified as 'Extreme' (steep). The north-facing slopes on the St. Anton side hold powder well after storms. Heliskiing is available on Mehlsack (2,652m) and Schneetäli (2,450m) with up to 1,200m of vertical descent, requiring state-certified guides. Local ski guides offer daily guided tours to minimize avalanche risk.
valueDescription: St. Anton is one of the more expensive Austrian ski resorts. A six-day adult Ski Arlberg lift pass costs approximately €370 in low season and €450 in high season (2025/26 prices). Accommodation prices range from around €1,100 per week for a compact apartment to over €165,000 per week for luxury chalets. The resort has a free bus service day and night, reducing transport costs. Compared to other Arlberg villages, St. Anton offers a wider range of budget accommodation options, but overall costs are higher than in smaller Austrian resorts like Söll or Bansko.
familyDescription: St. Anton has a dedicated beginners' area on the lower slopes of Gampen, with nursery slopes and gentle blue runs served by practice lifts. The Hopplweg slope at Gampen is a child-friendly themed run. However, the overall terrain in St. Anton skews steep, with over 40% of marked runs classified red or black. Families with young children often base themselves in Lech or Oberlech, which have more extensive beginner terrain and car-free villages. St. Anton's ski school offers English-speaking group lessons for children, and the pedestrianised village centre is walkable.
apresSkiDescription: St. Anton has a lively après-ski scene centered on bars like Mooserwirt, Krazy Kanguruh, and Basecamp, which fill up from 3 PM. The Steissbach valley run ends at several boisterous bars. For a quieter evening, restaurants such as Verwallstube and Hospiz Alm offer traditional Tyrolean dining. The village also has wine bars and fine dining options. Evening skiing is available on Wednesdays and Fridays. The atmosphere is known for being sociable and energetic, with a mix of party bars and more refined venues.
resortCharacterDescription: St. Anton is a traditional Tyrolean village with a pedestrianised centre featuring painted facades and cobbled streets. Cars are banned from the centre, and a free bus service connects accommodation to the lifts. The village has grown around skiing, with modern lift infrastructure integrated into the historic setting. It is larger and more strung-out than other Arlberg villages, with accommodation spread along the valley. The architecture is a mix of traditional alpine buildings and modern hotels, giving it a lived-in character rather than a purpose-built feel.
liftSystemDescription: The St. Anton sector is served by 38 lifts (part of 85 across Ski Arlberg), including the Galzigbahn funitel gondola, the Valluga cable cars (Valluga 1 to 2,650m and Valluga 2 to 2,811m), and the Rendlbahn gondola. The Flexenbahn gondola (completed 2016) links St. Anton to Lech and Zürs. Lifts are modern and high-speed, with heated chairlifts and gondolas. The system is efficient, with queues rare outside peak weeks. The highest lift is the Valluga 2 at 2,811m, which requires a guide for skiers.
linkedResortsDescription: St. Anton is part of the Ski Arlberg, Austria's largest linked ski area, connecting St. Christoph, Stuben, Lech, Oberlech, Zürs, Warth, and Schröcken via lifts and pistes. The Flexenbahn gondola (2016) links St. Anton to Lech and Zürs, and the Auenfeldjet connects Lech to Warth-Schröcken. The total linked area offers 300 km of groomed slopes and 200 km of off-piste, served by 85 lifts. The Run of Fame circuit (85 km) traverses the entire area from St. Anton to Warth and back. The Ski Arlberg pass covers all villages and also includes the Sonnenkopf area via bus.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Col de Rousset consists of 26 runs spread across an elevation from 1,255 m to 1,700 m. The terrain is dominated by beginner and intermediate runs, with 46% beginner, 29% intermediate, 18% advanced, and 7% expert according to OnTheSnow. There are two dedicated beginner areas: one in the heart of the resort with small green runs served by a carpet, a snow line, and a small ski lift, and another on the Beure plateau with a second snow line. The resort also features a snowpark and a boardercross for freestyle enthusiasts. The longest run is 1.3 km, and total skiable terrain is 22 km. The slopes are set between forests and cliffs, offering panoramic views over the Diois valley.
valueDescription: Col de Rousset offers good value for a small family resort. A family pass costs €79.60 per day for up to four people (2 adults and 2 children under 18, or 1 adult and 3 children), with additional children at €13 each. Daily adult lift tickets are around €18 on weekdays and weekends, with half-day tickets at €16. A six-day adult pass is €115.90. The resort also offers a snow shuttle from Die for €9 round trip, making it accessible without a car. Season passes (€231 for adults) also grant access to the nearby resorts of Font d'Urle and Lus-la-Jarjatte. Children under 5 ski free.
familyDescription: Col de Rousset is family-friendly with several dedicated facilities. The ESF (French Ski School) offers a Kindergarten for children aged 6-9 and a Piou-Piou initiation club for ages 3-5. There are two beginner areas with carpets and snow lines. Non-ski activities include a rail sled, tubing, dog-sledding, and sledging runs. The resort provides off-bag rooms with microwaves and toilets, one in the heart of the resort and one at the Beure car park. A snow shuttle from Die (€9 round trip) makes access easier for families without a car. Children under 5 ski for free, and the family pass allows four people to ski together at a reduced rate.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Col de Rousset is low-key and centered on the resort's dining options. The resort has bars and restaurants serving sweet and savoury crepes, traditional dishes like gratin de ravioles and tartiflette, and hot and cold drinks. At the Beure car park, L'échappée Belle offers ravioles, daily specials, soups, and waffles. A snack bar and bread store operate within the resort. For more variety, the village of Saint-Agnan-en-Vercors (8 km away) has a bakery, bar, and restaurant, while La Chapelle en Vercors or Die (30 minutes by car) offer additional restaurants, bars, and amenities. There is no mention of nightclubs or lively bars.
resortCharacterDescription: Col de Rousset is a small, family-oriented resort situated at a mountain pass in the Vercors High Plateaux Nature Reserve. The resort is set between forests and cliffs, with panoramic views over the Diois valley. It was developed on former communal pastures and has a functional layout with ski-in/ski-out access from accommodation. The resort consists of a central area with shops, restaurants, and lift bases, plus an upper car park at Beure. The architecture is not described in detail, but the setting is natural rather than purpose-built in a traditional alpine style.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 11 lifts: one high-speed detachable quad chairlift and 10 surface lifts (including 6 téléskis and 1 téléski école). The chairlift provides access to the main slopes, while surface lifts serve beginner areas and upper sectors. Lifts operate from 9:00 to 17:00 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: The Col de Rousset season pass also provides access to the nearby resorts of Font d'Urle and Lus-la-Jarjatte, both in the Drôme region. Additionally, the Grande Traversée du Vercors, a 29 km groomed cross-country trail, connects Col de Rousset to Vassieux and Font d'Urle, linking the resorts for Nordic skiing.

terrainDescription: The Steinplatte Winklmoosalm ski area sits on a sunny high plateau at 1,100–1,900 m. The terrain consists of wide, groomed pistes, with 85% snowmaking coverage. The slopes are predominantly south-facing, providing good visibility and soft snow in the afternoon. The ski area offers 42 km of pistes, with a mix of easy and intermediate runs, and a few difficult runs. The longest run is 9.5 km. The slopes are groomed daily and rarely crowded.
offPisteDescription: The Schwarzlofer Pasture provides deep-snow slopes for freeriders. There are 3 km of marked ski routes and some gladed areas for off-piste skiing. The off-piste terrain is limited but offers opportunities for powder skiing after fresh snowfall.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €66 (2025/26 season). On Super Tuesdays in January and from 10 March 2026, the day ticket is reduced to €41. The ski area is part of the 3-Country Leisure Arena, SuperSkiCard, and Snow Card Tirol networks, offering multi-resort options. Ski rental for 6 days starts at around 750 DKK (approx. €100). The mountain huts offer fair prices for food and drink.
familyDescription: The Steinplatte is a certified Tyrolean family ski region. The Triassic Kinderland at the mountain station offers childcare for children aged 3 and above, with half-day and full-day options. The Hausberg Arena in Waidring provides a free beginner area for children up to age 6. The Triassic Funline features dinosaur-themed elements and a wave track. The Triassic Park includes a 1 km winter hiking trail, indoor playrooms, and a Mama Lounge for warming up. The ski school in Waidring offers lessons for children. The resort is car-free in the village and has a free shuttle bus.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is accessed from the village of Waidring, a traditional Tyrolean village, and from Reit im Winkl in Germany. The Steinplatte plateau itself is a high-altitude sunny plateau with modern lift infrastructure. The base villages retain alpine charm with traditional architecture. The resort is purpose-built for skiing with gondolas and chairlifts, but the villages offer a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a modern 15-person gondola from Waidring and an 8-seater gondola from Seegatterl. The new Bäreckbahn is a detachable 6-seater chairlift from Doppelmayr with heated seats, weather protection hoods, and automatic bar locking. There are 14 lifts in total, including chairlifts and surface lifts. The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:30, with gondolas until 17:00. Queues are rare outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: The Steinplatte ski area is linked with the German Winklmoosalm ski area, accessible with one ski pass. It is also part of the 3-Country Leisure Arena, which includes eight ski resorts across Tyrol, Salzburg, and Bavaria. Additionally, it is a member of the Snow Card Tirol and SuperSkiCard networks, allowing access to multiple resorts.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of four runs on the east flank of the Sternstein mountain, with a vertical drop of 288 metres. The runs are classified as easy to medium difficulty: the Familienabfahrt (1,050 m) is easy, while the Standardabfahrt (1,600 m), Slalomhang (1,550 m), and Schlepplifthang (800 m) are medium. The slopes are groomed and suitable for beginners and intermediates. There is also a dedicated children's area with a 174-metre magic carpet.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs €46, with reduced rates for youths (€42) and children (€28). The resort is small and family-run, offering good value for beginners and families looking for a low-cost ski day without the crowds of larger resorts. Parking is free at the base station.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family ski area, with a dedicated children's zone called ELEFUNTASIA featuring a fairytale castle and snow-spitting elephants. The children's area covers 4,000 m² and includes the longest magic carpet in Upper Austria at 174 metres. A ski school offers lessons for children and beginners. The easy Familienabfahrt (1,050 m) provides a gentle progression from the nursery slopes. The base station has ample parking and a restaurant.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is limited: there is one bar/pub and one restaurant at the base station. The resort offers floodlit touring evenings on Tuesdays and Thursdays until 21:00, where the family run and drag lift are open for uphill and downhill. The Sternsteinhof restaurant stays open later on those evenings for self-service.
resortCharacterDescription: The Sternstein ski area is a small, traditional local ski hill in the Mühlviertel region of Upper Austria, near the town of Bad Leonfelden. It is not a resort village but a day-trip area with a base station, parking, and a single restaurant. The setting is on the forested east flank of the Sternstein mountain, with views of the Bohemian Massif. The atmosphere is low-key and family-oriented, lacking the alpine charm of larger resorts but offering convenience for local skiers.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three lifts: a combined 8-seater gondola and 6-seater chairlift (Kombibahn) that takes skiers from the base at 814 m to the summit at 1,102 m, a T-bar lift serving the intermediate runs, and a 174-metre magic carpet in the children's area. The total lift capacity is 2,850 persons per hour, which is sufficient for the small ski area. Queues are rare. The lifts operate daily from 08:30 to 16:15.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located on the north side of the Kitzbüheler Horn, opposite the Wilder Kaiser range. It consists of three main sectors: the Eichenhof lifts (quieter, with long t-bars), the Harschbichl and Hochfeld lifts (busy with ski schools), and the Penzing-Bauernalm area (steeper slopes). The terrain is dominated by wide, meandering runs through forest, with 40% easy, 58% intermediate, and 8% difficult slopes. Notable runs include the long red Lacknerabfahrt and Eichenhofabfahrt, the blue Penzingabfahrt for relaxed cruising, and the single black Saureggabfahrt (piste 7) which is steep and tight. Most runs face north and north-west, helping to hold snow. The vertical drop is 945 m from 1,604 m to 659 m.
offPisteDescription: Within the St Johann ski area, off-piste opportunities are limited to inter-piste powder fields in the forested slopes after a fresh dump. The resort's off-piste terrain is rated 2.86/5 by users. However, the nearby resort of Fieberbrunn, part of the Schneewinkel area and about 15 minutes by car, is renowned for freeriding and hosts the Swatch Freeride World Tour. Fieberbrunn receives 50% more snow than Kitzbühel in an average season and offers a range of off-piste terrain from gentle slopes to challenging ski touring routes. The Steinplatte area also provides additional off-piste diversions.
valueDescription: St Johann in Tirol is considered a budget-friendly option compared to more famous Austrian resorts like Kitzbühel. A local adult day pass costs approximately €55, with a 6-day pass around €275. Children aged 6–15 receive a 50% reduction, and under-6s ski free. Family passes with additional discounts are available. Accommodation ranges from budget guesthouses and apartments to mid-range family-run hotels, often at lower prices than in neighbouring resorts. The resort also offers good-value dining, with many mountain huts serving traditional Tyrolean fare at reasonable prices.
familyDescription: St Johann is particularly well-suited to families, earning a 4.60/5 rating for family friendliness. There are three free nursery areas at the base of the Eichenhof lifts, the Hochfeld lifts, and near the Bauernalm cable car, each with gentle slopes and free t-bars. Ski schools (Tyrolean Ski School, Wilder Kaiser Ski School, Eichenhof Ski School) offer English-speaking instruction and children's courses. The resort's mascot, Yappy Yapadu, appears on the slopes. The village is compact and walkable, with most accommodation within a few minutes of the lifts. Off-slope activities include toboggan runs, ice skating, and an indoor playground (Murmi's Kinderland) in nearby Kirchdorf.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in St Johann is sociable but low-key, with a friendly and relaxed vibe. The pedestrianised town centre has over 50 bars and restaurants. Popular evening spots include Max Pub, Michi’s, and The Rock Bar. The 300-year-old Huber Bräu brewery is a notable attraction. The scene is not as wild as in Ischgl or Sölden; it is more about comfortable bars and cafes. For a livelier night out, Kitzbühel is a 10-minute train ride away. Mountain restaurants like the Angerer Alm (with a 6,000-bottle wine cellar) also offer evening dining.
resortCharacterDescription: St Johann in Tirol is a historic market town with a traditional alpine character, not a purpose-built resort. The town centre features colourful buildings, a baroque church (Dekanatspfarrkirche Maria Himmelfahrt), and a lively main square. The pedestrianised zone attracts around 20,000 visitors per day. The resort has a year-round feel, with a mix of shops, cafés, and cultural events. The town's history dates back to Celtic times, and it was a summer residence for the bishops of Chiemsee. The architecture is predominantly traditional Tyrolean, with wooden chalets and family-run hotels.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern and efficient, with fast gondolas and chairlifts covering the main Harschbichl sector. Total lifts: 22 (3 gondolas, 5 chairlifts, 14 surface lifts). The Eichenhof area still uses long t-bars, which can be challenging for boarders. Snowmaking covers 80% of the ski area, ensuring reliable conditions. Free ski buses connect St Johann with neighbouring resorts in the Kitzbüheler Alpen. Lift efficiency is rated 4.32/5 by users. The resort also has a heated chairlift option.
linkedResortsDescription: St Johann is part of the Schneewinkel ski area, which includes Oberndorf, Kirchdorf, Erpfendorf, Fieberbrunn, St. Ulrich-Hochfilzen, Waidring-Steinplatte, and Reit im Winkl, offering 176 km of pistes accessible from a 3-day lift pass at no extra cost. Free ski buses connect these villages. The Kitzbüheler Alpen AllStarCard provides access to 2,750 km of pistes across 22 ski regions, including Kitzbühel, SkiWelt, Saalbach, and Zell am See. The resort is also well connected by train (8 minutes to Kitzbühel) and by car to SkiWelt and other areas.

terrainDescription: The Stubai Glacier ski area spans five glaciers with 65 km of pistes and 21 km of ski routes between 1,695 m and 3,210 m. Terrain is dominated by wide, gentle blue cruisers on the glacier, with a 10 km valley run called Wilde Grub’n descending 1,500 vertical metres from the Wildspitz summit (3,210 m) to the valley station (1,750 m). The Daunhill slope has a 60% gradient, and there are 13 GPS-tracked off-piste runs in the Powder Department. The Gamsgarten area at 2,620 m offers family-friendly blues, and the Stubai Zoo snowpark at 3,000 m+ has multiple freestyle lines. Most runs face north-east, holding snow well into spring.
offPisteDescription: The Powder Department provides 13 GPS-tracked off-piste runs with safety information and avalanche transceiver training checkpoints. There are 21 km of marked but ungroomed ski routes, and extensive backcountry access beyond the marked area. The glacier’s high altitude (above 2,900 m) and north-east aspect help preserve powder for several days after a storm. Freeride skiing and ski touring are popular, with avalanche safety equipment recommended.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost approximately €57 when booked online (€62 at the ticket office), and a 6-day adult pass is around €224. Children under 10 ski for free when accompanied by a paying parent. A 4+ day pass automatically upgrades to the Stubai Skipass, covering four ski areas in the valley at no extra cost. The SKI plus CITY Pass includes 12 resorts plus Innsbruck attractions. Free ski buses run from valley villages to the glacier base station, and there are 1,800 free parking spaces at the valley station. Innsbruck Airport is 45 minutes away, with budget flights from the UK.
familyDescription: The BIG Family programme includes a dedicated Kinderland at Gamsgarten (2,620 m) with a covered magic carpet, slalom course, and a giant snow mammoth. Children under 10 ski for free with a paying parent. The BIG Family Ski Camp offers childcare from age 3, and there are English-speaking ski schools at Gamsgarten. The free ski bus and valley accommodation in Neustift and Fulpmes make logistics straightforward. The Gamsgarten area has a family restaurant and a fun slope with bumps and jumps.
apresSkiDescription: On the glacier, the Schaufelspitz restaurant at 2,900 m is the Alps’ highest award-winning restaurant, serving regional Tyrolean cuisine. The Jochdohle at 3,150 m is Austria’s highest mountain restaurant, and the Dresdner Hütte offers traditional alpine hut food. In the valley, Neustift and Fulpmes have traditional Tyrolean bars and restaurants, with the Alpensporthotel Mutterberg at the base station providing the liveliest après. Innsbruck, 45 minutes away, offers full city nightlife. The scene is relaxed and Tyrolean rather than wild.
resortCharacterDescription: Stubai Glacier itself is a day-skiing destination with no overnight accommodation on the mountain; most visitors stay in the Stubaital valley villages of Neustift, Fulpmes, and Schönberg. These are traditional Tyrolean settlements with painted churches, farms, and family-run hotels, not purpose-built resort blocks. The glacier base station has a modern gondola terminal, parking, and rental shops, but the character is that of a working alpine valley rather than a ski-in/ski-out village. The free ski bus connects all villages to the glacier.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 26 lifts, including the new €60 million Eisgratbahn 3S gondola that carries 3,000 people per hour up to 2,900 m in under 12 minutes. There are 5 gondolas, 7 chairlifts, and 10 surface lifts, plus 4 moving carpets. The Schaufeljochbahn provides access to the Top of Tyrol viewing platform at 3,210 m. Lifts are modern and efficient, with daily grooming of all open pistes. Queues are rare except on strong November weekends, when additional car parks and buses are used.
linkedResortsDescription: Stubai Glacier is one of four bus-linked ski areas in the Stubaital valley. The others are Schlick 2000 (25 km of treelined slopes, 10 lifts), Elfer (5 km of slopes plus 3 toboggan runs, one floodlit), and Serles (6 km of beginner-friendly slopes). A 4+ day Stubai Skipass covers all four areas, and the SKI plus CITY Pass includes 12 Innsbruck-area resorts plus city attractions. Free ski buses connect the valley villages to each ski area. The combined total is about 144 km of pistes, though the areas are not lift-linked.

terrainDescription: The ski area stretches from the village of Bayrischzell up to Sudelfeld and on to Voglsang at 1,563 meters. The mostly wide, expansive slopes in the Mangfall Mountains offer views of the Bavarian Alps, Inn Valley, and Kaiser Mountains. The terrain includes 31.5 km of groomed slopes: 8 km easy (25%), 18.7 km intermediate (60%), and 4.8 km difficult (15%), plus 1.9 km of ski routes. The longest runs are from Bergstation Vogelsanglift to Bayrischzell (4 km) and from Oberes Sudelfeld to Bayrischzell (4.2 km). A funslope with a children's race track is on the Wedellift, and a freeridecross with wave runs and steep curves is available. A snow park with obstacles for snowboarders and freeskiers is also present.
valueDescription: A day ticket for adults costs €53, youth €46, and children €26. On Wednesdays from 7 January 2026, all guests ski for €37 (except Ash Wednesday). A family super ski pass is available on Sundays, and there is a ladies day. The Sudelfeld ski area is part of the Alpen Plus association, so multi-day tickets are valid at Brauneck, Wallberg, Spitzingsee, and Zahmer Kaiser. The MVV-Skipass Sudelfeld offers combined train and ski pass from Munich. Free parking is provided for ski pass holders. A skibus from Munich (Wednesdays and Saturdays) includes the skipass: €70 adults, €62 youth, €56 children, €41 toddlers. The resort has 2,100 free parking spaces for skipass customers.
familyDescription: The resort has two children's areas: Snuki-Kinderland and Kinderland am Tannerfeld. Snuki-Kinderland features a magic carpet and rope lift, and the Kinderland has a winter play arena with archways and waves. There are flat, child-friendly beginner lifts and five ski schools offering courses for all ages. A ski kindergarten is available. The Waldkopfbahn 6-seater chairlift has a heated ski depot for families. Free skibus from Bayrischzell station to the slopes runs every 30 minutes in the morning. Parking is free for skipass holders. The resort is rated among the top 15 most family-friendly in Germany.
apresSkiDescription: There are 5 bars and pubs in the resort. The Sport Alp in Bayrischzell has a bistro with après-ski. Rustic alpine huts and mountain guesthouses offer culinary delights on sunny terraces. A winter jamboree with snowtubing on the Tannerfeld and floodlit night tubing provide additional evening entertainment. The resort has 15 restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Bayrischzell is a traditional Bavarian village with 1,691 inhabitants, located at the foot of the Wendelstein mountain in the Mangfallgebirge. The ski area is accessed from the village via a chairlift. The resort has retained much of its original natural charm, with a unique natural landscape. The village has a heated open-air swimming pool, tennis courts, mini golf, and an ice rink. The railway from Munich to Bayrischzell has been operating since 1911. The Wendelstein cable car from Osterhofen provides access to the Wendelstein ski area.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 13 lifts: 7 chairlifts, 5 drag lifts, and 1 cable car (Wendelstein cable car). The modern 8-seater Sudelfeldkopf chairlift, one of the fastest in Germany, was installed in 2017/18 and connects Grafenherberg to Sudelfeldkopf at 1,436 m. The 6-seater Waldkopfbahn chairlift has a heated ski depot and rental station. Other lifts include 4-seater chairlifts and surface lifts. The total capacity is 19,000 persons per hour. Operating hours are 08:30 to 16:30. The lift system is modern and efficient, with heated seats and weather protection hoods on the main chairlifts.
linkedResortsDescription: The Sudelfeld ski area is part of the Alpen Plus ski pass association, which includes the nearby resorts of Brauneck, Wallberg, Spitzingsee, and Zahmer Kaiser in Tyrol. Multi-day tickets are valid on all 75 mountain railways and lifts in these areas. The MVV-Skipass Sudelfeld offers combined access with Munich public transport. Free skibuses connect Bayrischzell, Fischbachau, Brannenburg, and Oberaudorf to the slopes. The Wendelstein ski area (10 km of slopes) is accessible via cable car from Osterhofen.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of three sectors (glacier, Langenstein, Rosim) offering 48.5 km of pistes. 19.5 km are easy, 12.9 km medium, 7.8 km difficult, and 8.3 km ungroomed. Runs include the 4.6 km Madritsch panoramic slope, the demanding Kanonenrohr, and the 2.7 km Rosim piste. Terrain is both above and below treeline, with forested sections providing shelter in bad weather. The longest run is 11 km with a vertical drop of 1,350 m.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes three ungroomed alpine descents: Hochleiten (3 km), Schaubachweg (2.5 km), and Marlet (2.8 km). Freeride descents from the Schöntaufspitze sector into the Rosimtal are popular but require a guide due to technical sections. An avalanche training camp is available near the Madriccio Mountain Hut. The deep-snow slopes are ideal for freeriders and telemark skiers.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate: a day ticket costs €51–60 for adults, with discounts for youth and children. A 6-day adult pass is around €320. Accommodation is available across budgets, with cheap hostels and pensions as well as luxury hotels. The village has 2,000 beds. Free parking and a free ski bus reduce costs. Self-catering apartments are common.
familyDescription: The resort is ranked #1 for family friendliness in South Tyrol. The Solda Ski School, operating for 75 years, offers children's courses and a Yeti Club ski kindergarten. Nursery slopes are located in the lower part of the area. Childcare facilities are available but limited. The village is quiet and safe for families, with a traffic-free feel.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is minimal. A few bars exist, including one at the glacier base station, but the village is quiet after dark. There is no club scene. Night skiing and tobogganing are available on the illuminated 2 km toboggan run three times a week. The overall atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Sulden is a traditional alpine village at 1,900 m, set in a remote valley at the foot of the Ortler. It has a population of 400 and 2,000 tourist beds. The village is spread out, with the main center not directly connected to the lifts. Some reviewers find it soulless, but the setting is spectacular with views of glaciers and three-thousanders. The architecture is typical South Tyrolean.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 11 lifts: a 10-person gondola (Rosimbahn), a cable car in two sections, six quad chairs, one triple chair, and two surface lifts. The new gondola replaced an old double chair, improving access to the Kanzel area. A horse-drawn sleigh connects the gondola base to the Langenstein chair. Lifts operate from late October to early May, with a capacity of 26,750 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Sulden is part of the Ortler Ski Arena, which includes nearby resorts such as Trafoi, Belpiano-Malga S.Valentino, Watles, and Val Senales. However, the three sectors within Sulden (glacier, Langenstein, Rosim) are not fully interconnected; they are linked by lifts and pistes but require some skating or a horse-drawn sleigh between them.

terrainDescription: The Tannheim-Zöblen-Schattwald ski area consists of multiple small, family-friendly sectors. The Zöblen-Schattwald sector has 15 km of pistes with 60% easy, 27% intermediate, and 13% difficult runs, including a FIS-homologated race track used by national women's teams from Switzerland and Japan for training. The Neunerköpfle sector above Tannheim offers panoramic slopes served by a gondola lift. Overall, the terrain is dominated by gentle, wide slopes suitable for beginners and leisurely skiers, with limited challenging terrain. The known facts indicate 66% beginner, 29% intermediate, and 4% advanced runs across the combined area.
valueDescription: Lift passes are relatively inexpensive compared to larger Austrian resorts. A day pass for the Zöblen-Schattwald area costs €53 for adults, with a six-day pass at €247. The Tannheimer Tal offers a 'SKI TANNHEIMER TAL' ticket covering 20 lifts and cable cars across the valley. Additionally, network passes like 'Superschnee' provide access to 500 km of slopes across eight ski areas in Tyrol, the Kleinwalsertal, and the Allgäu, including 35 mountain railroads and 200 lifts. Special family passes and grandparent discounts are available, making it a budget-friendly option for families.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families with young children. All sectors have dedicated children's areas with magic carpets, ski carousels, and gentle practice slopes. The Pumuckl slope in Schattwald-Zöblen features a course and ski carousel for little ones. Ski schools offer courses from age three, and a free ski bus connects the villages, reducing the need for car travel. The gentle terrain and short routes make it easy for children to progress, and the valley's quiet atmosphere suits family holidays.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and family-oriented. The ski huts in Schattwald and Zöblen serve local delicacies and provide a relaxed atmosphere for an afternoon break. In Tannheim, a few bars and clubs stay open late, but the overall scene is quiet rather than lively. The valley's focus is on outdoor activities rather than nightlife, with mountain restaurants offering early evening dining.
resortCharacterDescription: The Tannheimer Tal is a traditional Tyrolean high mountain valley, described by ADAC as the 'most beautiful high valley in Europe'. The villages of Tannheim, Zöblen, and Schattwald retain an alpine character with wooden chalets and a peaceful atmosphere. The valley is not purpose-built; it has evolved from farming communities into a family-friendly holiday destination. The setting is surrounded by the Allgäu Alps, with lakes and forests, offering a picturesque natural environment.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of modern installations across the valley. The Neunerköpfle sector has a gondola lift, while Zöblen and Schattwald use chairlifts and surface lifts. Total lift count is 10 across the combined area. Early Bird Ski Fun allows skiing from 8:00 a.m. in some sectors. A free ski bus connects the villages, reducing the need to drive between lifts. Queues are generally short due to the small scale of the area, and the lifts are well-maintained.
linkedResortsDescription: The Tannheim-Zöblen-Schattwald area is part of the larger Tannheimer Tal ski network, which includes four ski areas: Neunerköpfle (Tannheim), Füssener Jöchle (Grän), Schattwald-Zöblen, and Nesselwängle. A free ski bus connects all villages. The 'SKI TANNHEIMER TAL' ticket covers 20 lifts and cable cars in the valley. Additionally, the 'Superschnee' ski pass provides access to 500 km of slopes across eight ski areas in Tyrol, the Kleinwalsertal, and the Allgäu, including 35 mountain railroads and 200 lifts. The 'Vitales Land' pass covers 70 cable cars and lifts in northern Tyrol and eastern Allgäu.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split between Monte Lussari and Monte Florianca, connected by a four-seater chairlift. The terrain is set within a 24,000-hectare state-owned forest, the largest in Italy. The most notable run is the Di Prampero, a 4 km black slope from Monte Lussari to Camporosso, which has hosted World Cup races and is illuminated for night skiing on Tuesdays and Fridays. The slopes face north, with a vertical drop of 1,002 m. The resort also offers 2 km of night skiing and a snow park for freestyle.
offPisteDescription: While Tarvisio's groomed runs are the main draw, the nearby Sella Nevea ski area, a few kilometres away, is a freeride paradise with 15 km of cross-border slopes (10.5 km in Italy, 4.5 km in Slovenia) between 2,000 and 1,100 m, offering spring skiing and off-piste terrain. The area is known for its powder and is popular with advanced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the cheapest in Friuli-Venezia Giulia, with a day pass for adults at €44 (2025/26 season). Accommodation options range from slopeside hotels to apartments in the town, with self-catering available. The resort is known for attracting school groups, indicating budget-friendly pricing. A six-day pass is around €189.50, and a season pass is €550.
familyDescription: Tarvisio is rated the top family resort in Friuli-Venezia Giulia by Skiinfo. The resort features Tarvislandia, a dedicated snow park for children with inflatables, a school field, snow tubing, a sledding area, a mini trampoline, and an 880 m bobsleigh track on rails. Ski schools offer lessons for all ages, and there are 60 km of cross-country trails suitable for families. The slopes are quiet and uncrowded, with fast lifts and no queues. Dog-sledding excursions are also available.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is quiet and relaxed, with around 5 bars and pubs and 2 clubs in the resort. Local restaurants serve Friulian and Austrian specialties such as frico (cheese and potato dish) and gulasch with polenta. For a livelier scene, visitors often cross into Austria or Slovenia, which are a short drive away. The resort also offers ice skating and spa facilities.
resortCharacterDescription: Tarvisio is a historic alpine town with a main square featuring the Saints Peter and Paul Church, medieval towers, and a mix of traditional architecture. The resort area is centered around the base of Monte Lussari, with slopeside accommodation. The mountaintop hamlet of Monte Lussari, with its pilgrimage church, is considered one of the most picturesque in the world. The town has a quiet, authentic atmosphere, not a purpose-built resort.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 13 lifts including a gondola, 6 quad chairs, a triple chair, a double chair, and 4 surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with a total capacity of 16,459 persons per hour. Queues are rare due to the quiet nature of the resort. The main gondola provides top-to-bottom access from the base to Monte Lussari. All slopes are covered by snowmaking.
linkedResortsDescription: Tarvisio is part of the Friuli Venezia Giulia Ski Area 3 Confini, which includes the nearby Sella Nevea area. Sella Nevea offers 15 km of cross-border slopes (10.5 km in Italy, 4.5 km in Slovenia) and is a freeride paradise. Additionally, the Austrian resort of Nassfeld is just over the border and can be combined with Tarvisio for a larger ski area. The Dreiländereck area is also nearby.

terrainDescription: The Cedars sits in a single high-alpine bowl with nine groomed runs, five of which are rated for advanced skiers. The terrain is set among ancient cedar forests, with wide, gentle slopes at the top that are ideal for beginners and families. The bowl faces north, helping preserve snow quality, and the compact layout means all runs are within easy reach of the base lifts. The resort's higher elevation (base around 2,100 m, summit up to 2,850 m) provides reliable snow cover through the season.
offPisteDescription: The Cedars offers accessible off-piste terrain in the high-alpine bowl and adjacent slopes, with fresh lines that remain untracked due to low crowd density compared to Mzaar. The higher elevation and north-facing aspects preserve snow quality, making it a popular choice for advanced skiers seeking powder. Off-piste enthusiasts can explore the bowl's edges and the open faces below the summit, though no marked off-piste routes exist and caution is advised due to variable snow conditions.
valueDescription: Lift passes at The Cedars are among the most affordable in Lebanon, with adult day passes around $30 on weekdays and $40 on weekends. Accommodation in nearby Bsharri village offers budget-friendly guesthouses and traditional lodges, often cheaper than staying on the mountain. Equipment rental is available at competitive rates (around $15-25 per day). The resort's smaller size and lower infrastructure costs contribute to its value, though amenities are more basic than at larger resorts like Mzaar.
familyDescription: The Cedars is well-suited for families, with gentle, wide slopes at the top that are ideal for beginners and children. The resort's serene atmosphere and traditional lodges provide a relaxed environment. Ski schools and equipment rental are available, and the nearby Bsharri village offers family-friendly accommodation and cultural attractions such as the Gibran Museum and the Qadisha Valley monasteries. The compact layout means parents can easily supervise children on the slopes.
apresSkiDescription: The Cedars has a minimal après-ski scene, with a few cozy chalets and restaurants in the base area serving Lebanese cuisine and traditional music. The real nightlife is in Beirut, a two-hour drive away. Most visitors enjoy a quiet evening in Bsharri village, where local eateries offer hearty mountain dishes. The resort's atmosphere is serene and family-oriented rather than party-focused.
resortCharacterDescription: The Cedars is a traditional, rustic ski resort set in a high-alpine bowl adjacent to the UNESCO-protected Cedars of God forest. The base area features historic lodges and a few modern facilities, but the overall character is natural and unpolished, with a focus on the surrounding cedar groves and mountain scenery rather than purpose-built infrastructure. The resort's first ski lift was installed in 1953, making it Lebanon's oldest ski area, and it retains a time-capsule charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at The Cedars consists of six lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts, serving a compact ski area of about 11 km of marked piste. The lifts are older and less modern than those at Mzaar, but they are sufficient for the resort's size. Queues are generally short except on peak weekends. The resort's first ski lift was installed in 1953, making it Lebanon's oldest ski area, and the current infrastructure has seen limited upgrades since.

terrainDescription: The Espace Killy ski area spans 300 km of marked pistes, with 22 green, 61 blue, 46 red, and 25 black runs. Val d'Isère's terrain is divided into three main sectors: Solaise offers long cruising blues, Bellevarde features steep blacks including the famous Face de Bellevarde, and Le Fornet provides access to off-piste terrain. Tignes contributes the Grande Motte glacier at 3,456 m with wide, gentle slopes at the top, and wooded runs around Les Brévières. Tignes also has long blue descents and expert routes like the black Sache run. The linked area includes two terrain parks and 44 km of cross-country skiing.
offPisteDescription: The Aiguille Percée sector above Tignes Le Lac features north-facing slopes that hold powder for days after a snowfall, with dramatic terrain and the famous rock arch. Val d'Isère's off-piste is particularly impressive around Le Fornet and La Daille, where couloirs and open bowls challenge experienced riders. The Col Pers and Pisaillas glacier areas offer serious backcountry for guided groups. The Chardonnet valley below Tignes also provides outstanding off-piste terrain.
valueDescription: Tignes offers better value than Val d'Isère, with a wider choice of mid-range apartments and more affordable restaurants. Accommodation costs are typically 20-30% lower in Tignes. Self-catering apartments dominate, keeping costs down. Lift passes are the same across both resorts, giving access to the full 300 km of pistes. For budget-conscious travellers or larger groups, Tignes provides significantly better value without compromising skiing quality.
familyDescription: Tignes has four dedicated beginner zones spread across Val Claret, Le Lac, Le Lavachet, and Les Brévières, offering gentle slopes and ski schools that cater to children and beginners in multiple languages. The resort's ski-in/ski-out setup means families spend less time walking and more time skiing. Off the slopes, the Lagon Centre features pools and slides. Val d'Isère also has nursery slopes in the village centre and snow gardens, and holds the Famille Plus label, but Tignes' layout is more convenient for families with young children.
apresSkiDescription: Val d'Isère offers one of the most iconic après-ski scenes in the Alps, with La Folie Douce on the Bellevarde slopes featuring live music and open-air shows, and village venues like Cocorico and Doudoune Club keeping the buzz going late. Tignes delivers a more laid-back but still vibrant offering, with popular venues including Loop Bar with its sunny terrace and live music, as well as DropZone and Le Moose for relaxed drinks and dancing. Dining in Tignes is diverse and generally more budget-friendly, with highlights including the panoramic Le Panoramic on Grande Motte and the Michelin-starred Ursus in Val Claret.
resortCharacterDescription: Val d'Isère is a historic Savoyard village with traditional stone-and-wood architecture, a baroque church, and narrow streets that predate ski tourism. It has a pedestrian-friendly centre with boutique shopping and fine dining. Tignes, by contrast, was rebuilt after the original village was submerged under the Lac du Chevril reservoir in the 1950s. The architecture is functional concrete from the 1960s and 70s, though recent renovations have improved comfort. The resort is split into several villages including Val Claret, Le Lac, and the more peaceful Les Brévières, which retains some old Savoyard character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 90 lifts including 2 funiculars, 4 cable cars, 4 gondolas, 44 chairlifts, and 36 surface lifts. A new high-speed six-seater chairlift serving the Aiguille Percée sector was installed for winter 2025-26, travelling at six metres per second and carrying up to 2,800 skiers per hour, replacing an older quad. The system is modern and efficient, with heated chairlifts and high-speed gondolas. The Val d'Isère side has 71 lifts including 37 chairlifts, 6 gondolas, and 3 cable cars.
linkedResortsDescription: Tignes and Val d'Isère are linked to form the Espace Killy ski area, with over 300 km of marked pistes and 90 ski lifts. The two resorts are connected by multiple lift and piste links, allowing skiers to move freely between them on a single lift pass. The area also includes the Grande Motte glacier, which extends the season and provides reliable high-altitude skiing.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Torgnon covers 13 km of pistes, with 19% beginner, 60% intermediate, and 21% advanced runs. The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with wide red runs accessed from the Col Fenêtre and Collet chairlifts. A single blue run from Chantorné provides easy cruising, while two black runs offer more challenging descents. The slopes are situated on a sunny, south-facing plateau with views of the Matterhorn. A snow park and a snow playground are available for freestyle and family fun.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass costs €39 (as of 2025), which is lower than many larger Aosta Valley resorts. The resort is also included in the Cervinia multi-day ski pass, offering additional value for those staying in Cervinia. Accommodation and dining in the traditional village are reasonably priced compared to purpose-built resorts.
familyDescription: Torgnon is well-suited for families, with a dedicated snow playground and a ski school offering patient instruction. The gentle blue run from Chantorné and the wide red runs provide safe progression for beginners. The slopes are calm and rarely crowded, and the compact village is easy to navigate with children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Torgnon is low-key, with a few mountain huts and village bars. Bar Ristorante La Vetta and Cafè La Vigne are popular spots for drinks and live music. The focus is on relaxed dining and enjoying the mountain atmosphere rather than a party scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Torgnon is a traditional Alpine village composed of 22 hamlets spread across a sunny moraine terrace. The architecture is typical of the Aosta Valley, with stone and wood buildings. The village is not purpose-built; it has a historic character with a 16th-century house and a parish museum. The setting offers panoramic views of the Matterhorn.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts, including an 8-seater cable car from Mongnod to Chantorné, a four-seater chairlift at Chantorné, a double chairlift at Col Fenêtre, and a four-seater chairlift at Collet. The main cable car has an external ski rack. The lifts are a mix of modern and older installations, with a total uphill capacity of 5,500 skiers per hour. Snowmaking covers 10 km of runs.
linkedResortsDescription: Torgnon is not directly lift-linked to other resorts, but it is included in the Cervinia multi-day ski pass, allowing skiers staying in Cervinia to access Torgnon's slopes without an additional ticket. The resort is a 30-minute drive from Cervinia. Similarly, the nearby car-free village of Chamois is also covered by the same pass.

terrainDescription: The local Torgon ski area offers 23 km of pistes, with 71% beginner and 29% intermediate runs. The slopes are wide and natural, with a vertical drop of 644 m from 1,335 m to 1,979 m. From the top of Plan de Croix, there are views of Lake Geneva, the Dents du Midi, and Mont Blanc. The terrain is generally mellow, with some steeper sections in the Torgon sector as noted by the Espace Liberté description.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste in Torgon is low-key but rewarding. The gentle, open terrain above the treeline holds powder longer than busier parts of the Portes du Soleil due to low skier numbers. There are tree runs in the lower sections, and the area is lift-accessible. The freeride is not technical but offers fun, low-risk lines after fresh snowfall.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are lower than in larger Portes du Soleil resorts. A day pass costs 51 CHF for adults, and a six-day pass costs 243 CHF. Accommodation in Torgon is generally affordable, with self-catering apartments and small guesthouses. The resort is less developed, keeping costs down.
familyDescription: Torgon is a small family-oriented resort with a dedicated beginner area at Jorette, featuring magic carpets and gentle slopes. The Swiss Ski School offers lessons for all ages. The village is quiet and safe for children, with a relaxed atmosphere. The resort is part of the Portes du Soleil, giving families access to a wide range of slopes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Torgon is very quiet. There are a few local restaurants and bars, such as Le Perroquet, and a nightclub, but the scene is low-key. Most visitors head to larger resorts like Châtel or Morzine for more lively options.
resortCharacterDescription: Torgon is a traditional Swiss mountain village with wooden chalets and a peaceful atmosphere. It sits on a sunny terrace above the Rhone valley, with views of Lake Geneva and the Alps. The resort has retained its authentic character with moderate development, unlike larger purpose-built stations.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Torgon has been reduced: the two main lifts from the village to the slopes have been permanently closed since 2016/17. Skiing is now accessed via the Plan-du-Croix sector, which has a quad chair and several surface lifts. The resort has 10 lifts in total, but access from the village requires a short drive or bus to the base area.
linkedResortsDescription: Torgon is part of the Portes du Soleil ski area, which links 12 resorts across France and Switzerland with 600 km of pistes and 202 lifts. It is also part of the smaller Espace Liberté area, which includes Châtel, La Chapelle d'Abondance, and Morgins, offering 120 km of slopes with 56 lifts. Direct connections from Torgon include the Tour de Don lift to Châtel and links to La Chapelle d'Abondance.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of four named slopes on the north face of Mount Olympus: the Zeus slope (380 m, advanced, FIS homologated), Hera (350 m, beginner), Aphrodite (150 m, beginner), and Hermes (150 m, intermediate). The total groomed piste is approximately 3 km, with a vertical drop of about 80 m from 1,930 m to 1,850 m. The slopes are short and compact, suitable for a day trip, and the resort is the southernmost ski destination in Europe.
valueDescription: Cyprus is one of the cheapest skiing destinations in Europe. A family of four can ski for a week in late February for a total of £474, including return flights, car hire, and second-hand equipment. Lift passes are inexpensive and can be purchased online or at the resort. Accommodation in nearby villages like Prodromos or Platres is affordable, and the resort itself offers equipment hire and day passes.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated baby lift on the Sun Valley slopes, serving the 150 m Aphrodite and Hermes runs, which are suitable for beginners and children. Ski lessons can be arranged on the day at the ski school huts near the hire building. The compact layout and short slopes make it easy for families to stay together, and the resort is less crowded than major Alpine destinations. The Sun Valley Baby Lift is specifically noted for children.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski resort is located on the north face of Mount Olympus, the highest peak in Cyprus, at an elevation of around 1,850–1,930 m. The base area consists of the Cyprus Ski Club building with a shop, hire facilities, and refreshment areas. The nearest villages are Troodos (3 km) and Prodromos (5 km), which offer accommodation and traditional Cypriot tavernas. The surrounding Troodos Mountains are known for Byzantine churches and forested slopes, giving the area a natural, non-commercial character. The resort is not a purpose-built ski village but a mountain-top facility with a laid-back atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of four lifts: one chairlift on the Zeus slope (380 m) and three T-bar surface lifts (Hera 350 m, Aphrodite 150 m, Hermes 150 m). All lifts are equipped with smart gates using chip-and-pin passes, which can be purchased at cash desks at Sun Valley and North Face locations. The lifts operate daily from 10:00 am to 5:00 pm during the season.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 43 km of pistes between 1,400 m and 2,205 m, with 14.5 km of blue runs, 25 km of red runs, and 3.2 km of black runs. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle slopes around Lake Turrach, suitable for beginners, and sporty red runs on the Kornock, Wildkopf, and Panorama lift sectors. The only black run is the 0.6 km Direttissima (piste 35). A 12 km circuit around the lake allows skiers to cross between Carinthia and Styria multiple times. The slopes are predominantly north-facing, with 44% north, 34% east, 6% south, and 17% west orientation. The highest point is at the Kornockbahn top station at 2,205 m, offering wide cruising runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available from the top of the Kornockbahn at 2,205 m, with open terrain on the soft, rounded Nockberge mountains. The backcountry offers opportunities for powder skiing, though no specific routes or couloirs are described in the sources. The area is within the Nockberge Biosphere Reserve, with natural snow conditions.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the 2025/2026 season: adult day pass €68, 6-day pass €363; child day pass €35, 6-day pass €182. Children under 6 ski for free. Multi-day tickets are valid throughout Carinthia and East Tyrol. Parking is free at the valley stations. Accommodation ranges from luxury hotels (Hotel Hochschober from €370/night) to mid-range (€150-200/night) and chalets (€200-400/night). The resort is small and uncrowded, with minimal lift queues, offering good value for a snow-sure Austrian resort.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with dedicated nursery slopes by Lake Turrach, served by the Übungswiesenlift, Ottifantenlift, and Mängländerlift. Children under 6 ski for free. Night skiing is available on Wednesdays from 19:30 to 21:00 on the Übungswiesenlift. The Nocky Flitzer alpine coaster (1.6 km) operates year-round. Ice skating is possible on the frozen Turracher See in winter. The Pistenbutler service provides small surprises and assistance on the slopes. Ski schools offer courses for children and adults, and there is a Kids' Slope and a Fun Mountain with a snow park.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the K-Alm and Sonnalm bars, and the Nockalm pavilion serving beer on tap. There are 5 bars and pubs and 2 clubs in the resort. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on family-friendly activities. Night skiing and night sledding (Tuesdays and Thursdays) provide evening entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: Turracher Höhe is a traditional mountain village on a pass summit at 1,795 m, straddling the border between Carinthia and Styria. The village is centered around Lake Turrach (1,763 m) and consists of two luxury hotels, over 30 small and medium-sized hotels and guest houses, and many vacation houses hidden in stone pine forests. There is no grocery store or gas station. An ecumenical church was built in 1985. The resort is located within the UNESCO Nockberge Biosphere Reserve, with soft, rounded mountain silhouettes. The atmosphere is calm and intimate, with a focus on nature.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 16 lifts: 6 six-seater chairlifts (Kornockbahn, Schafalmbahn, Zirbenwaldbahn, Panoramabahn, Turrachbahn, Wildkopfbahn), one combined chairlift and gondola (Panoramabahn), 8 drag lifts, and various training lifts. The hourly transport capacity exceeds 19,400 people, which is high for the size of the ski area, resulting in minimal queues. The lifts are modern, with the Wildkopf chairlift being a recent addition. The Kornockbahn is the main artery to the summit at 2,205 m. The Panoramabahn is accessible for guests with disabilities and families with strollers.

terrainDescription: The ski area Ginals-Unterbäch offers 20 km of marked pistes, predominantly blue and red runs. 63% of pistes are classified as beginner, 21% intermediate, and 16% advanced. The slopes are wide and uncrowded, with a vertical drop of 1,322 m from base at 1,228 m to summit at 2,550 m. The terrain includes a Swiss Snow Kids Village with a magic carpet, ski carousel, seesaws, jumps, and rails. The highest lift reaches 2,550 m, providing views of the Bietschhorn.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the cheapest in Valais at CHF 49 per day for adults and CHF 33 for children. Accommodation options range from budget hostels and guesthouses to upscale hotels, with a wide variety available. The resort offers ski-in/ski-out chalets and apartments. A free ski bus connects Unterbäch to Eischoll and Bürchen for pass holders.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a Swiss Snow Kids Village offering a magic carpet, ski carousel, and small terrain features. The ski school provides lessons for children and adults. The slopes are mostly gentle (63% beginner) and uncrowded. A free ski bus connects Unterbäch to Eischoll and Bürchen. Night skiing is available on Tuesdays during the main season from 4:30 to 6:30 p.m. for CHF 5.00.
apresSkiDescription: The village has two bars that often feature live music. There are five restaurants serving local specialties such as raclette, rösti, and Valais wine. The après-ski scene is relaxed rather than lively, with a focus on dining and drinks.
resortCharacterDescription: Unterbäch is a traditional Valais mountain village situated on a sunny high plateau at 1,200 m. It retains its alpine character with historic buildings, a church, and a municipal library. The village is not purpose-built; it has a genuine Swiss village atmosphere with local shops and restaurants. The entire Turtig/Wandfluh region is designated as part of the Inventory of Swiss Heritage Sites.
liftSystemDescription: The combined Unterbäch-Eischoll area has 11 lifts, including chairlifts, gondolas, and surface lifts. The main chairlift to Brandalp operates daily from 9:00 to 16:30. The aerial cableway from Raron to Unterbäch provides access from the valley, with cabins running every 30 minutes. Lifts are modern and queues are rare. Snowmaking covers 50% of the slopes with 60 machines.
linkedResortsDescription: Unterbäch is linked to the neighboring resort of Eischoll via the Strygge-Erez ski lift and connecting pistes, forming a combined ski area of 35 km of pistes and 7 lifts. The resort is also part of the Magic Pass, which provides access to 69 winter destinations in the region, including areas in Upper Valais, Disentis-Sedrun, Andermatt, and the Bernese Oberland.

terrainDescription: The resort has 41 marked runs: 3 green, 20 blue, 14 red, and 4 black, with a vertical drop of 800 m. The local valley offers mostly gentle blue runs, while the connection to Pra Loup introduces steeper red and black slopes. A dedicated beginner area with magic carpets and a small chairlift provides safe learning space. The upper slopes are above 2000 m, offering open terrain. A terrain park is available for snowboarders.
valueDescription: Lift passes for the Espace Lumière area cost €45 per day for adults and €38 for children (5–17) for the 2023–2024 season. The resort offers free shuttles between villages and slopes. Accommodation is available close to the pistes, with ski-in/ski-out options. Self-catering apartments and residences are common, keeping costs moderate compared to larger purpose-built resorts.
familyDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux holds the Famille Plus Montagne label, with a snow garden, toboggan runs, a children's village, and a play library in the resort centre. There is a 600 m² ice rink, a cinema, and the Verdon Express rail toboggan run. Ski school offers lessons for all ages, and the resort has a daycare for children from 1 to 5 years old. The traffic-free village centre allows families to walk everywhere in boots.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 15 restaurants and 6 bars. The après-ski scene is quiet, with a few bars and restaurants in the village centre. A cinema, ice rink, and toboggan run provide evening entertainment. High-altitude terraces offer dining with views. A nightclub is also available. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: La Foux d'Allos is a purpose-built resort with mostly wooden buildings, avoiding large concrete complexes. Accommodation is ski-in/ski-out or a short walk from the slopes. The village is small and convenient, with all amenities within walking distance. It blends authenticity and modernity, set in the Mercantour National Park.
liftSystemDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux has 17 lifts: 7 drag lifts, 8 chair lifts, and 2 magic carpets. The lift system is a mix of older and newer installations, with some slow chairs and drags, but recent upgrades have improved efficiency. Queues are rare outside peak weeks. The link to Pra Loup can close in high winds.
linkedResortsDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux is part of the Espace Lumière ski area, which links it to Pra Loup and Val d'Allos Le Seignus, offering a combined 180 km of slopes with 120 lifts. The connection to Pra Loup is via the Agneliers valley, subject to snow conditions. The linked area includes 136 marked runs and serves a total of 33 lifts across the whole Val d'Allos resort.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans 70 km of groomed trails between 1,550 m and 2,737 m, with north-facing slopes predominantly above treeline. The Arrondaz gondola lifts skiers to a high plateau with wide green and blue runs, while the upper mountain via chairlift offers access to Punta Bagna's couloirs and powder fields. The longest run, 'Le Jeu', descends over 10 km from summit to village. On-piste advanced runs are limited; the black run 'Souches' into the village is the most challenging. Several blue runs are deceptively steep and could be rated red. Flat traverses exist, particularly at the start of Jeu and around Plateau d'Arrondaz, which can be challenging for snowboarders.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is abundant and high-quality, centered on Punta Bagna (2,737 m) with north-facing couloirs and bowls on both sides. Short hikes from the summit open additional backcountry playgrounds. The north aspect preserves snow quality, and the 'retours d'est' weather pattern brings dry, deep snow from Italy. Access to the best freeride lines requires local knowledge and challenging exits. The Refuge du Thabor area is a notable off-piste route. Speed riding, a hybrid of paragliding and skiing, originated here and remains popular.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable: a day ticket for Valfréjus and La Norma costs €42.50 for adults (2025/26), with a 5% discount for online purchase. A six-day adult pass is around €225. Accommodation is reasonably priced, with many self-catering apartments and a hostel (BodyGo Hostel) near the Charmasson chairlift. The combined pass with La Norma (135 km) adds value. However, children aged 12 and over are charged adult rates. Dining and groceries are moderately priced, though some reviews note high supermarket costs in the village.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with two dedicated beginner areas: the sheltered Chamasson chair near the village and the Plateau d'Arrondaz mid-mountain. A Kid Park on the Arrondaz plateau features children's games, a merry-go-round, and a snow castle. A 2 km Bob Park toboggan run provides off-slope fun. Ski schools (ESF, ESI) operate at the foot of the slopes, and the compact pedestrian village makes logistics easy for families. Free magic carpets are available at the beginner areas.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and centered on village bars. The Morricone and Rock n' Beer, both near the Charmasson chairlift base, offer extensive beer selections. On-mountain dining is limited to two restaurants, including La Bergerie. Village restaurants serve Savoyard cuisine. The scene is relaxed rather than rowdy, with no large nightclubs. Live music is occasional.
resortCharacterDescription: Valfréjus is a purpose-built resort founded in 1983, designed to blend into the forest with wooden and stone chalets. The village is compact and pedestrianized, with most accommodation ski-in/ski-out. It has a modern lift system and a family-friendly atmosphere, lacking the historic alpine charm of older villages but offering convenience and integration with nature. The village centre is traffic-free, with shops, restaurants, and services within walking distance.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern, with most major lifts replaced between 2009 and 2013. It comprises 1 gondola (Arrondaz) and 4 chairlifts, with a total capacity of 17,480 people per hour. The lifts are efficient and comfortable, with minimal queues outside peak weeks. However, some surface lifts remain older. The resort has 9 lifts in total (1 gondola, 4 chairs, 4 surface lifts).
linkedResortsDescription: Valfréjus is linked to La Norma via a common ski pass and free shuttle every 45 minutes, giving access to 135 km of pistes. It is also part of the Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise, allowing access to Aussois, Val Cenis, Bessans, and Bonneval-sur-Arc with a 6-day pass (1 free day each). The combined area offers over 550 km of slopes across the region.

terrainDescription: The ski resort has 8.3 km of slopes split across easy (46%), intermediate (29%), and difficult (25%) runs, situated between 1,460 m and 1,729 m elevation with a vertical drop of 269 m. The ski area offers views of the plateau and peaks of the Majella National Park, and also features a cross-country ski trail. Pescocostanzo village is noted as one of the most beautiful villages in Italy with a beautiful old town and ancient craft tradition.
familyDescription: The ski area includes a rope tow/baby lift called Lupetto with a length of 109 m and carrying capacity of 400 passengers per hour, suitable for beginners and children. The resort has ski-in/ski-out accommodation available.
resortCharacterDescription: Pescocostanzo is described as one of the most beautiful villages in Italy, featuring a beautiful old town and ancient craft tradition. The village offers various winter activities beyond skiing, including cross-country skiing on a trail that is one of the most popular in the region.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts with a total capacity of 4,217 passengers per hour and total lift length of 1.7 km. The main chairlift Vallefura was installed in 2009 by LEITNER, measuring 929 m with a carrying capacity of 2,010 passengers per hour. Additional lifts include the 2006 Valle Gelata 2-person chairlift (358 m), the 1977 Monte Calvario J-bar (341 m), and the Lupetto rope tow/baby lift (109 m).

terrainDescription: The ski area on the Dachberg is accessed by an 8-seater gondola from the village to Gadastatt at 1,817m, where a practice area (Kleinvalser Arena) with a conveyor belt and platter lift serves beginners. Three T-bar lifts ascend from Gadastatt to nearly 3,000m, offering wide intermediate cruising runs and a blue slope from the Faltschona drag lift. The valley run is a 2.5km advanced descent; most skiers take the gondola back down. The terrain includes high alpine runs with views from the Bernina massif to the Valais 4,000m peaks, and the longest run is 10km over a vertical drop of 1,630m.
offPisteDescription: Freeriders can access powder snow slopes away from the pistes, with the high summit altitude (nearly 3,000m) and reliable snowfall providing good conditions. The area is described as having abundant freeride terrain, with untouched powder off-piste that can remain fresh for days after a storm. The off-piste includes high alpine slopes around the Dachberg and is a key draw for the resort, which markets itself as a freeride destination.
valueDescription: Vals is considered the best value ski resort in Switzerland, with an adult day lift pass costing CHF39 for the 2025/26 season and children under 16 skiing for free. The gondola from the village to the mountain is free of charge. A season ticket costs CHF399. These prices are among the most affordable in the Alps, and the resort offers fair pricing for lift passes and accommodation.
familyDescription: The Kleinvalser Arena at Gadastatt (1,817m) features a conveyor belt and platter lift for beginners, plus a pony lift for children. The Swiss Snowsports School Vals offers children's ski school, private and group lessons. The gondola is free for all, making access easy for families. The valley run is difficult, so families can take the gondola down. Ski rental is available at the valley station and in the village.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with a few cozy bars and mountain restaurants such as Bergrestaurant Gadastatt, Dachberghütte, and scHIRMBAR. The main evening attraction is the world-famous Therme Vals thermal spa, a listed architectural masterpiece of stone and light, open until late. Nightlife beyond dinner and drinks is limited, with the village offering a relaxed, tranquil atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: Vals is a traditional, picturesque Swiss village with a population of 946, featuring Walser architecture with timber-rich dwellings and sharply pitched roofs. The village centre, Vals Platz, lies secluded behind two gorges, accessed via a single valley corridor. It is not purpose-built; the historic character is complemented by the modern Therme Vals spa (built 1996 by Peter Zumthor) and the surrounding alpine landscape.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one modern 8-seater gondola from the village to Gadastatt (500m vertical), three older-style T-bar lifts from Gadastatt to the Dachberg summit (remaining 1,130m vertical), and two conveyor belts/platter lifts for beginners at Gadastatt. The gondola is free. Operating hours are 9:00–16:30 for the gondola and 9:15–15:45 for the upper lifts. The system is small but efficient for the terrain.

terrainDescription: The ski area is centered on the Hochjochferner Glacier, with slopes from 3,212m down to 2,011m. The terrain is dominated by wide, smooth glacier cruising runs above the treeline, suitable for intermediates. The standout feature is the 8 km long Smuggler Run (Schmuggler Abfahrt) valley descent, dropping 1,200m vertical. Lower mountain runs like Teufelsegg and the black-rated Leo Gurschler slalom training piste offer more challenging terrain. The piste distribution is approximately 35% easy (blue), 45% intermediate (red), and 20% difficult (black), with a total of 27–42 km of marked runs depending on the source. Most runs are above 2,000m, with few below the treeline.
valueDescription: Lift pass pricing varies by season: early glacier season (Sep–Nov) adult daily €68, mid-season (Nov–Dec) €49, low season (Jan–May except holidays) €56, and peak holiday periods €62. A 6-day adult pass ranges from €282 to €313. Children (2–14) pay €32–40 daily, and the Snowkids ticket allows children born 2016 or later to ski for €5 per day when accompanied by a parent with a full-price pass. A points-based Schnupperkarte is available for beginners: 20 points for €36 (10 rides on beginner lifts). Half-day passes are also offered from 12:30.
familyDescription: The resort has dedicated beginner areas at Lazaun and Glockenlift, with two magic carpets, a children's carousel, and an Indian tipi-themed children's course. Ski school offers courses for children and adults. The Snowkids ticket makes family skiing affordable: children born 2016+ pay only €5/day with a parent holding a full-price pass. The practice slopes are separate from main runs, providing a safe learning environment. The village of Kurzras is compact and car-free, with ski-in/ski-out access from many accommodations.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is centered on the small village of Kurzras (Maso Corto) at 2,011m, at the end of the Val Senales valley. It is a quiet, authentic alpine settlement rather than a purpose-built resort, with just a few hotels and Europe's highest hotel, the Glacier Hotel Grawand at 3,212m. The atmosphere is sporty and minimalist, popular with professional ski teams for training. The valley has a primitive, timeless beauty, far from the crowds of larger resorts. Accommodation options are limited but unique, including a caravan park at altitude.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 11 lifts: one gondola (the Schnalstal Glacier Cable Car, inaugurated in 2024 with 80-passenger cabins and cabrio modality, ascending from Kurzras to 3,212m in 6 minutes), two quad chairlifts, one triple chairlift, three double chairlifts, and four surface lifts. The cable car is modern and efficient, while the chairlifts serve the glacier and lower mountain. Lift queues are minimal due to the resort's small size and capacity of 15,500 persons per hour. The system is well-maintained and suitable for the compact ski area.

terrainDescription: Val Thorens sits at 2,300m in a vast natural cirque dominated by six glaciers. The terrain is mostly above the treeline, with long sweeping runs that suit intermediates particularly well. Nursery slopes are located right in the village for first turns, while long cruising reds link down to Les Menuires. Most slopes face north, protecting them from spring sunshine, and many border glaciers with year-round snow. The Val Thorens-Orelle sector offers about 150 km of pistes with 60 runs, including wide glacier runs and steep faces like Combe de Caron.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes glacier couloirs off Pointe de Thorens, steep chutes on Combe de Caron and Cascades, and a vast freeride domain around the Glacier de Thorens and the Orelle Valley. Freeriders get more frequent mid-winter powder top-ups than most other resorts. The area has a world-class snowpark spanning 90,000 m² with jumps, rails, and airbags.
valueDescription: Val Thorens carries a premium for its altitude and snow reliability, with the 3 Vallées lift pass being expensive (€409 for a 6-day adult pass in 2025-2026). However, a cheaper local Val Thorens-Orelle pass is available, covering about 150 km of slopes and costing only about 10-25% less than the full area pass. Accommodation ranges from ski-in/ski-out apartments to luxury hotels, with dining options from affordable casual eateries to Michelin-starred restaurants. Self-catering apartments can help manage costs, but overall the resort is pricier than nearby Les Menuires.
familyDescription: Val Thorens has four nursery slopes accessed via free magic carpet lifts near the resort, with a low-cost Mini ski pass for the extended beginner area. ESF-run snow gardens and private nanny services are available. The Lagon sports centre offers slides, trampolines, and a dedicated toddler fun park. The resort is pedestrianized and compact, making it easy to walk between accommodation and slopes. However, the high altitude can be cold and windy, and there are no tree-lined slopes for shelter on bad weather days.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski starts on-mountain at La Folie Douce, then continues in the village at clubs like Malaysia and Saloon Bar, known for high-energy parties that last late into the night. Bar 360 is another popular venue. Dining ranges from Michelin-starred restaurants such as L’Oxalys and Le Diamant Noir to casual Savoyard eateries. The scene is lively and vibrant, with a focus on partying rather than quiet relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Val Thorens is a purpose-built resort established in 1972, sitting at 2,300m above the treeline. The village is compact and car-free, with most lodgings clustered around the central Place Caron and opening directly onto the snow. Pedestrian zones and a free shuttle service make it easy to navigate. The architecture is modern concrete apartment blocks, lacking alpine charm but offering ski-in/ski-out convenience. The high exposed location can be bleak, windy, and very cold.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Val Thorens is modern and state-of-the-art, with fast, high-capacity lifts including the Cime Caron cable car reaching 3,200m and the Bouchet lift at 3,230m. The Val Thorens-Orelle sector has about 30 lifts. However, strong winds can force lift closures due to the exposed high-altitude location. The 3 Vallées area as a whole has 183 lifts with a capacity of 260,000 skiers per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Val Thorens is part of the Les 3 Vallées ski area, the largest lift-linked ski area in the world with 600 km of pistes and 183 lifts. It connects to Les Menuires, Saint-Martin-de-Belleville, Méribel, Courchevel, and Brides-les-Bains via lifts. Additionally, the Orelle resort in the Maurienne valley is directly linked to Val Thorens via the 3 Vallées Express gondola (5,122m long), providing access from the other side of the mountain. The combined Val Thorens-Orelle sector offers 150 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: The Valtournenche ski area covers 54 km of pistes, with 17 km easy, 35 km medium, and 2 km difficult. The slopes above the Salette gondola terminal at 2,245m are mostly gentle blue runs, well above the treeline. Two chairlifts provide uplift to further straightforward blues, with one linking to the more challenging upper sector and the Cime Bianche col connection to Cervinia. The terrain is predominantly undemanding and intermediate-friendly, with wide, flowing runs ideal for relaxed cruising.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste in Valtournenche benefits from the high-altitude terrain and links to Cervinia and Zermatt. The Theodul Glacier offers some of the best snow in the region. A guide is essential due to the high mountain terrain. Heli-skiing is available. It is illegal to ski off-piste in Italy without full avalanche safety equipment.
valueDescription: Valtournenche is less expensive than neighbouring Cervinia, offering a more budget-friendly base for accessing the same ski area. Lift pass prices for the local Valtournenche area are €43 for one day, €121.50 for three days, and €227 for six days.
